steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject that belongs to 'you' in the phrase لَكُمُ (belongs to you)
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make it a noun meaning one day
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific day
Adding fatha at end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Active participle form showing who/what has this quality. This word describes the state of dominion/power (الملك) mentioned in the ayah.
Changed from singular (one) to plural (many) by adding ين at the end, showing multiple victorious ones instead of just one
Added the final vowel mark (fatha) to complete the word's grammatical case
This is a basic preposition showing location or place. In this ayah, it shows where they are dominant/appearing (ظٰهِرِينَ).
The ي at the end changes to ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make them pronounceable
Adding 'al' makes it specific
The kasra at the end shows this word follows فِى (in) in the ayah
This is a question word asking about who will help. It connects to the verb يَنصُرُنَا (will help us) that comes after it.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from what was said before - like saying 'so' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adds ي at start and changes middle vowel to show ongoing/present action instead of past
Adding 'na' means 'us'. The word 'us' is the object (the one receiving the help) in this sentence.
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something. It's like saying 'from' or 'of' in English.
Added the ِ mark under the م to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'min'
Added a small ن mark at the end to show this word connects to the next word بَأْسِ (might/punishment). Together they mean 'from (Allah's) might/punishment'
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the basic noun
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something after it (اللَّهِ - of Allah)
Added ال to make it specific and changed the form slightly to create the unique name for The One True God
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and added shadda on ل. The word بَأْسِ (might/punishment) possesses this word, making it mean 'Allah's might/punishment'

steps.m: if (introduces a condition in إِن جَآءَنَا - if it comes to us)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a possibility. In this ayah, it connects two ideas: 'if it comes to us' with what comes after. Think of it like the word 'if' in English when you say 'if it rains, I'll bring an umbrella.'
Added vowels and the weak letter ا replaced ي. This makes it past tense.
The long alif adds emphasis to the coming action
Added نَا at the end to show who received the action (us)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense. This word shows what Pharaoh (فِرْعَوْنُ) did.
The و (weak letter) changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Adding vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of this foreign word that became part of Arabic
Adding damma (ُ) at the end shows this is the subject doing the action in this sentence - Pharaoh is the one who 'said' (قَالَ)
Basic form of the word that means 'what' or 'that which'. This word doesn't come from a root - it's a special word used to ask questions or connect ideas.
Added fatha (َ) on م to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'ma'

steps.m: what/that which (object of أُرِيكُمْ - I show you)
steps.n: Changed alif to alif madd (آ) to show a longer 'aa' sound. In this ayah, this word is being used to refer to what Pharaoh is showing/telling his people.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he saw' to 'was shown' - like changing from doing the seeing to being shown something by someone else

steps.m: I show you all (in response to فِرْعَوْنُ)
steps.n: The word changed from 'I show' to 'I show you all' by adding كُمْ (kum) at the end to indicate Pharaoh is speaking to multiple people
This is a basic particle used to make exceptions in Arabic. It's like saying 'but' or 'except' in English.
The shadda and alif are added to strengthen the meaning and connect it to what follows. In this ayah, it introduces 'the path' (سَبِيلَ) as the exception.
This is the basic negative particle that negates the verb that comes after it. In this ayah, it negates the verb أُرِيكُمْ (I show you).
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
Adding hamza prefix makes the verb causative
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Added hamza prefix to make the verb causative (showing something to someone). The final ي changes to ى due to being a weak letter.
Added dagger alif (ٰ) to help show pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a negation particle that makes the verb يَنصُرُنَا (help us) negative, changing it to 'will not help us'
The و connects this negation to the previous statement, showing these ideas are linked together
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning
This is the basic concept of showing someone the right way
Added vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he guided' in the past tense
Removed 'ha' sound and changed middle vowel to 'i' to make it present tense 'guide'
Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action
Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being guided (you all)

steps.m: except (what follows أُرِيكُمْ - I show you)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that restricts or excludes what comes after it from what comes before it. Here it connects to أُرِيكُمْ (I show you) to mean 'I only show you' or 'I show you nothing except'

steps.m: to travel or follow a path

steps.n: Basic noun pattern showing a path or way
Adding ي makes it mean 'a path' - this is a common pattern for nouns describing ways or means

steps.m: the path (being guided to by أَهْدِيكُمْ)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this is the path being guided to in the phrase 'I guide you to the path'
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the concept of guidance itself
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda

steps.m: the right guidance (possessed by/connected to something)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something after it
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha marks
Changed to common past tense form by adding alif

steps.m: and he said (introducing what the believer said to his people)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this to the previous statement. This introduces the words of the believer to his people.
Added kasra to show the basic pronunciation of this relative pronoun
Added ال at the beginning to make it definite. This creates the basic relative pronoun used to connect parts of sentences.
Added shadda on ل and fatha marks to show proper pronunciation
Changed to Quranic spelling with wasl alif (ٱ), alif maqsura (ى), and elongation mark (ٓ). This word refers to the person who believed in the following verb ءَامَنَ (believed).

steps.m: to feel safe or trust in something

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he believed (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Changed the first letter to hamza with madd (ءَا) and added alif after it. This makes the meaning causative - instead of just feeling safe yourself, you're putting your trust in something else, which is what belief means.
Added vowel marks to form the noun 'people' - those who stand together as a community
Added يٰ at the beginning to call out to the people directly

steps.m: O my people! (addressing the speaker's community)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show the speaker's connection to the people being addressed
This is a special word used to emphasize what comes after it. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence.
Added ي at the end to show 'I' am speaking. This connects to the next word أَخَافُ (I fear) where I am telling what I'm afraid of.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the ي to show how to stretch the sound when reading. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle letter و changes to ا, which is common in Arabic words with و in the middle.

steps.m: I fear (the speaker in this ayah is the believing man)
steps.n: Changed to present tense first person (I) by adding أَ at the beginning and ُ at the end
This is a preposition with no root - it's a basic tool word in Arabic that shows something is on top of or over something else
Added ي with sukun (ْ) to connect with the next part
Added كُم which means 'you all'. This word is what the speaker fears for (object of 'I fear')
Forms a noun indicating similarity or likeness
Adds fatha to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Word is already in its final form with kasra, sukun and fatha
Connected the letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning 'day'

steps.m: day (possessed by what comes after it)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to and owned by what comes after it in the sentence
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a singular noun
This is a plural form that changes the word structure to show multiple groups
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it follows the word يَوْمِ (day of)

steps.m: to be like or similar to

steps.n: Added vowels to make a noun meaning 'likeness' from the basic idea of being similar
The fatha at the end shows this word is describing how something is similar to 'the way of the people of Noah' mentioned next in the ayah

steps.m: to persist, continue, or work diligently

steps.n: Changed from action to noun meaning 'usual way of doing things'. This word describes the characteristic behavior of قَوْمِ (people) mentioned after it.
Added kasra at the end because this word follows مِثْلَ (like) and comes before قَوْمِ (people), forming the phrase 'like the way of the people'
Changed root meaning into a noun for a group of people who stand/establish together. The word قَوْم comes from the idea of people standing together as one community.
The -i sound at the end (قَوْمِ) shows this word is connected to Noah's name after it, making it mean 'people of Noah' instead of just 'people'
This is a proper noun with no Arabic root

steps.m: Noah's (like the way of Noah's people)
steps.n: The tanween (double kasra ٍ) shows this word belongs to something else. Here it's connected to قَوْمِ (people) from the phrase 'like the way of Noah's people'
This is a proper noun naming an ancient Arab tribe mentioned in the Quran. The root meaning of returning/visiting became the name of this tribe.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the verse, like saying 'and'

steps.m: and Aad (name of ancient tribe, mentioned after قَوْمِ نُوحٍ)
steps.n: Added a kasra mark (small diagonal line) under the last letter to show it connects with 'the people of Noah' mentioned just before it

steps.m: Thamud (ancient tribe mentioned in قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ)
steps.n: This is a proper noun referring to an ancient Arabian tribe. In this ayah, it's mentioned alongside other ancient peoples like the people of Noah and 'Ad.
The fatha ending shows this is in the accusative case, following the same pattern as the other tribes mentioned
The و at the start means 'and', connecting this tribe to the previous ones mentioned (عَادٍ)
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that describes people or things
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
The ين ending shows this refers to multiple people
The و at the start means 'and', connecting this to the previous phrase about the people of Noah, Ad, and Thamud
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to the word بَعْدِ (after) to mean 'from after'.
Added a small noon mark at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Changes verb 'to be far' into the noun 'after' by adding specific vowel marks
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added هِمْ at the end to show this means 'after them'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُرِيدُ (he wants) negative, changing it to 'he does not want'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This connects the previous statement about earlier peoples with the statement that Allah does not want injustice.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'
Shows this word is the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start. The middle letter و changed to ي, making it easier to say. This word connects to ظُلْمًا (wrongdoing) that comes after it.

steps.m: to wrong, oppress, or act unjustly

steps.n: Changed from an action (to wrong) into a thing (wrongdoing) by adding the vowels ُ and ْ
Added ًا to show this is what Allah does not want/intend in this ayah. It's like saying 'Allah does not want wrongdoing' where 'wrongdoing' is what's being acted upon.
Connected the root letters with basic vowels to form singular noun
Changes singular 'servant' to plural form showing multiple servants
Added kasra to show this word follows a preposition
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular servants
Added the preposition ل with kasra to show purpose or direction
The ل merged with another ل to make a stronger L sound
The root letters combine with vowels to form a noun meaning 'people' - derived from the idea of people standing together as a group
The kasra at the end shows possession - in this context, it means 'my people'
The يٰ at the beginning is used to call out to someone - like saying 'Hey!' or 'O!'

steps.m: And O my people (who is speaking to إِنِّىٓ)
steps.n: The و at the beginning connects this phrase to what comes before it, while the speaker is connected to the following phrase 'Indeed I (إِنِّىٓ)'
This is a special particle that adds emphasis to statements. It's like adding 'truly' or 'certainly' in English.
Added ي at the end to show 'I' am doing the action. This word connects to أَخَافُ (I fear) in this ayah.
The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top to show it's elongated when reading. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into past tense form
Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' and ُ at end to show present tense. This word is connected to يَوْمَ ٱلتَّنَادِ (Day of Calling) as what is being feared.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or against something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting to the pronoun. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon you all (referring to the people being warned in this ayah)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to. This connects to the earlier part where the speaker says 'O my people' (يٰقَوْمِ)
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و becomes silent with sukun.
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb أَخَافُ (I fear) - it's what is being feared.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'called' to present tense 'call' by adding ت at start
Changed from a verb (calling) to a noun (mutual calling) by adding ال. The ت gets doubled because it combines with the ل of ال. Used after the word يَوْمَ (day of)
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun 'day'

steps.m: on the day (when تُوَلُّونَ you will turn away)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this is describing when something happens. It connects to the next word تُوَلُّونَ (you will turn away).
Added basic past tense form with doubled middle letter to show intensity of turning away
تُ at start changes it from 'he turned away' to 'you turn away'
Adds plural masculine ending to indicate multiple people doing the action

steps.m: turning back (describes someone doing the action)
steps.n: The مُ at the beginning and ِ in the middle work together to describe someone doing the action of turning back

steps.m: turning their backs (describes the state of the people in يَوْمَ تُوَلُّونَ - the Day when you turn away)
steps.n: The ين ending makes this describe multiple people. This word explains how people are turning away on the Day mentioned.
This is a negative particle that removes or denies what comes after it. In this ayah, it's saying there is no protector from Allah.
Starting with the basic preposition ل which shows direction or purpose
Added the pronoun كم to show who the preposition is directed to. In this ayah, it's warning about having no protector from Allah.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something coming from or originating from somewhere
The shadda (ّ) on م shows emphasis in pronunciation, which happens when certain letters come before من
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this connects to the next word (اللَّهِ) in the ayah
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
Added 'al' to make it specific/definite - like changing 'a god' to 'the god'
Added kasra at the end to show it means 'of the god'
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to the word عَاصِمٍ (protector) to show there is no protection from Allah.
Added ا after first letter and vowels to show who does the protecting. This word comes after مِنْ (from) in the ayah.
Added ٍ at the end to show this is any protector (not a specific one) and follows the word مِنْ (from)
Combination of و (and) with من (who)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from 'lost way' to 'makes someone lose way'. The يُ at start shows it's happening now. The ِ at end connects it to مَن before it.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel marks
Added ال with special spelling (ٱ), doubled the ل with shadda, and added ُ at the end to show this is the subject. This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before. In this ayah, it shows what happens after Allah leads someone astray.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added ه with damma to show this is 'for him'. The ه is a pronoun meaning 'him'.

steps.m: for him (connected to مِنْ هَادٍ - any guide)
steps.n: Added a small و mark (ۥ) to help with pronunciation. This word connects to مِنْ هَادٍ (any guide) in the ayah, showing that there is no guide for him.
This is a basic preposition that shows separation or distance. In this ayah, it shows there is no protection coming from Allah for the disbelievers on that day.

steps.m: to guide or show the right way
steps.n: The root letters show the basic idea of guiding or leading someone
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action
Changed final ي to tanween kasra to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a guide' instead of 'the guide'). This word follows مِنْ (from) in the ayah.
This is the base particle that shows certainty about what follows
Adding ل at the beginning adds extra emphasis to show something is definitely true
Adding و at the beginning connects this phrase to what came before it
The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb. Added fatha vowels to show past tense.
The alif is written with a hamza to show strong emphasis on 'coming'
Added كُمْ at the end to show the action was done to multiple people (you all)
This is a proper noun, the name of the prophet Joseph. As a non-Arabic proper name, it does not follow standard Arabic morphological patterns.
Added damma marks (ُ) to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. The name Yusuf is the one who 'came' (جَآءَ) in this verse.
This is a basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. Here it connects with قَبْلُ (before) to show that something happened 'from before' or 'previously'
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'before'
Adding damma at the end shows this word is in the nominative case
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'clear proof'
Added ات to make it feminine plural, showing multiple proofs
Added ال to make it definite (specific proofs)
Added بِ at beginning to show 'with/by' and kasra at end because it follows the preposition بِ

Starting with the basic interrogative/negation particle ما which can mean either 'what' or 'not' depending on context. In this ayah, it means 'not' because it's being used with زِلْتُمْ (ceased) to say 'you did not cease'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. Together with زِلْتُمْ it forms the phrase 'then you did not cease'

steps.m: to cease or stop being in a state

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he ceased' to command form 'cease!'. The middle letter ا drops out in commands.

steps.m: you all continued (to be in doubt)
steps.n: Added تُمْ to show many people (you all) did this action in the past
Basic preposition showing location or state. Here it connects to the word شَكٍّ (doubt) to show being 'in doubt'

steps.m: to be uncertain or have doubts

steps.n: The doubled ك shows strong/intense doubting
The word changed to show 'in doubt' because it comes after the word فِى (in). The dots under the last letter show this connection to فِى.
Combined the two words مِن (from) and مَا (what) together
The ن merges into the م, making it stronger (shown by the ّ mark). This happens because it's easier to say مِّمَا than مِنْمَا
Connected the root letters ج-ي-أ with the vowel marks َ (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he came'
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding ا after ج, with hamza moving to end
Added كُم to show the action was directed towards multiple people
Added kasra to show how the letter is pronounced
Added هِ to show who/what is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to يُوسُفُ (Yusuf).
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the word meaning 'until'
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning
Adding ى shows the meaning extends to what follows
The small alif (ٰ) and hamza (ٓ) show this connects to the word إِذَا (when) that follows it, making the complete meaning 'until when'
Basic time reference particle that points to a specific moment
Adding ا changes it from 'at the time' to 'when' for something that happened in the past. Here it tells us what happened when Yusuf died.
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This refers to when Prophet Yusuf died, as mentioned in the context of the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make the command form. The middle weak letter (ا) drops out in this form.
Added تُمْ to show that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past
This is a particle that strongly negates future actions. Here it negates the verb يَبْعَثَ (send), indicating that Allah will definitely not send another messenger.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to subjunctive mood, often used after 'that' or 'so that'
Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' by adding ال and merging letters. This word is unique and specifically refers to Allah.

steps.m: Allah (the subject of يُضِلُّ - leads astray)
steps.n: Added a strong L sound (shadda) and U sound at the end to show Allah is doing the action in this verse
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. In this ayah, it connects to the word بَعْدِ (after) to mean 'from after'.
Added a small ن (called tanwin) after the final ن. This is just a pronunciation helper and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be after, follow, come later

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to the root letters to make the word mean 'after'
Added هِ (meaning 'him') at the end. The small ۦ is just to help with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (sending) to a noun (person who sends/delivers)
The tanween alif shows this is the object of the verb يَبْعَثَ (send) in the ayah
Basic demonstrative word meaning 'this'
Added لِ and changed vowels to make it mean 'that' (pointing to something far) instead of 'this' (pointing to something near)
Added كَ at the end which completes the word 'that'
Added كَ at beginning to mean 'like/similar to'. This word describes how Allah leads astray (يُضِلُّ) in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: to stray/deviate from the right path

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Doubling the ل changes meaning from 'going astray' to 'causing others to go astray'
Adding alif at start makes the meaning causative - showing someone causing the action to happen to someone else
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This word shows Allah causing others to go astray.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns

Changed to the unique name of God by: 1) Adding ٱل (the), 2) Merging the ل with the first root letter creating ٱللّ with shadda, 3) Adding ُ to show this is the subject doing the action of sending (يَبْعَثَ) in this verse
This is a relative pronoun that connects to what comes after it. Here it refers to the person who is مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ (transgressing and doubting).

This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it refers back to the person being described as مُسْرِفٌ مُّرْتَابٌ (wasteful and doubting). The هُ shows it's about 'he' and the وَ helps complete the pronoun.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: one who goes beyond proper bounds (describes مَنْ هُوَ - whoever is)
steps.n: The verb 'went beyond bounds' becomes 'one who goes beyond bounds' - like changing 'ran' to 'runner'. Describes who Allah leads astray in the ayah.
Changes from verb 'to doubt' into the noun 'doubt'
Changed from the basic word for 'doubt' to Form VIII to show someone who has doubts, like adding '-er' in English (e.g. doubt → doubter). Describes مسرف in the ayah.
The doubled م makes the meaning stronger, like saying 'really doubting' or 'full of doubt'
Added tanween to show this is indefinite
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun that points to someone or something. It's like saying 'who' or 'which' in English.
Added ال at the beginning to make it more specific, like changing 'who' to 'the one who'
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word is the subject (doer) of يُجٰدِلُونَ (they dispute) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he argued back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Added alif to show the action is done between two parties

steps.m: he argues back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they argue back and forth (with others) (subject of ٱلَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word describes what ٱلَّذِينَ (those who) are doing.
Basic preposition showing location or place. In this ayah, it shows where the arguing is happening - in/about Allah's signs.
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'
Changed ي to ى with elongation mark (ٓ) for proper Quranic spelling. In this context, it shows what they are arguing about - concerning Allah's signs.

steps.m: to show, make clear, be apparent

steps.n: Adding ة turns this into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Added ات to make it plural, showing multiple signs or verses
Changed to genitive case with kasra because it's possessed by Allah in the phrase 'signs of Allah'
Changed the root into the basic noun form meaning 'a deity'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows the ل is doubled.

steps.m: of Allah (possessed by/connected to what comes before)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word before it (آيٰتِ/signs). The hamza at the start changed to a special connecting hamza (ٱ).

steps.m: to change or be different from something

steps.n: Basic noun form showing something being different or other than something else
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word سُلْطٰنٍ (authority) that comes after it
Added بِ at the beginning which usually means 'with', but when combined with غَيْرِ it means 'without'

steps.m: to have power or control over something

steps.n: Root letters got vowel marks (u and sukoon) to make a noun meaning 'power'
Adding ان makes the word stronger - like going from 'power' to 'absolute power'
Changed the alif to an alif maqsurah (ٰ) which is just a writing variation
Added tanween kasra to show this is the object of بِغَيْرِ (without)
Added fatha vowels and changed the final ي to ى to show past tense for 'he'
Added alif superscript (ٰ) to indicate long vowel sound
Added هُمْ at the end to show who was affected by the action (them)
The ُ (damma) in the middle shows this is about having a natural quality
Added vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he hated'
Changed the verb into a noun by putting a sukun on the middle letter

steps.m: intense hatred (object of كَبُرَ - it became great)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this word is the object that became great in the phrase 'it became great as intense hatred'

steps.m: to be present, to be near
steps.n: Basic letters showing the concept of presence or nearness
Connected the letters together to form the basic preposition
Added vowel marks to show it's a preposition connecting to the word after it (Allah)
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the basic noun form meaning 'deity'
Changed from the general word for 'a god' to the special name 'Allah' by adding ال and joining letters together
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word

steps.m: near or in the presence of
steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make the word readable
Adding the conjunction و to connect this phrase with previous text
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it more specific and definite. The ّ (shadda) emphasizes the ل sound.

steps.m: those who (subject of يُجٰدِلُونَ - they dispute)
steps.n: Added نَ at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word connects to يُجٰدِلُونَ (they dispute) in the ayah, describing the people who are doing the disputing.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif after first letter changes meaning from basic trust to religious belief
Adding وا at the end makes it refer to a group instead of one person
This is the basic demonstrative word meaning 'this'
Added ل with kasra to change 'this' to 'that', showing something farther away. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called dagger alif
Added ك at the end to make the complete word meaning 'that' - ك is part of the basic word, not just for emphasis
Added كَ at the beginning to mean 'like' or 'similarly'. In this ayah, it describes how Allah seals the hearts - He does it 'like that'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters

steps.m: he seals (subject of Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: The يَ at the beginning shows this is happening in the present. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject doing the action. This word refers to Allah sealing the hearts of the arrogant.
Changed the root into the basic noun form meaning 'a deity'

The word for 'god' (إِلَه) becomes Allah (ٱللَّهُ), the special name for the one true God. The ال makes it specific, like saying 'THE god' but it's more than that - it becomes a unique name. The ُ at the end shows Allah is doing something in the sentence.
This is the basic form showing the concept of being high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition that shows one thing is positioned above another

steps.m: upon, on, over (preposition connecting to قَلْبِ)
steps.n: The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is the final form. This word connects to قَلْبِ (heart) in this ayah, showing what is being acted upon.

steps.m: to be complete or surround entirely

steps.n: Added damma and doubled the middle letter to create a word meaning 'every' or 'all'. This word describes قَلْبِ (heart) in this ayah.
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to قَلْبِ (heart) that comes after it

Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the noun 'heart' - this pattern often turns action words into concrete nouns. The heart is called this because it can flip emotions and change directions.

steps.m: heart (possessed by مُتَكَبِّرٍ - an arrogant one)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this heart belongs to or is connected to the word after it (مُتَكَبِّرٍ - an arrogant one)

steps.m: to be large, great or proud
to act arrogantly, to be haughty
steps.n: Added ت at start and doubled middle letter to show someone making themselves act in this way

steps.m: one who makes themselves act proud/great
steps.n: Added مُ at the start to show 'someone who' and changed the verb pattern to make it a describing word for a person who acts proudly
Added ٍ at the end to show this describes the heart (قَلْبِ) mentioned in the ayah
Adding shadda and alif creates an intensive active participle showing someone who does this action habitually or with great force
The tanween kasra shows this word describes قَلْبِ (heart) in the phrase قَلْبِ مُتَكَبِّرٍ (heart of an arrogant person)
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce this name correctly. The ِ (kasra) under ف and ْ (sukun) on ر are essential pronunciation marks.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show that Pharaoh is doing the action - he is the one who 'said' (قَالَ) in this verse
This is a proper name, likely of ancient Egyptian origin, referring to Pharaoh's minister

steps.m: O Haman! (being directly addressed by Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show Pharaoh is calling out to Haman directly

steps.m: O Haman! (being directly addressed by Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added diacritical marks: 1) ٰ over ه and م to show elongation, 2) ُ at the end to show this is the subject being addressed
This is not a command form - بْنِ means 'son of' when used in names
Adding ٱ at start makes it a proper command form
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for me (connected to أَبْلُغُ - I may reach)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'. This word connects to أَبْلُغُ (I may reach) to show what Pharaoh wanted to build the tower for.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning a tall, prominent structure
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being built. This tower is what Pharaoh commanded Haman to build.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added emphasis by doubling the first letter
Added ي with kasra before it to show this refers to 'I'. This word connects to أَبْلُغُ (I reach) in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: to reach or arrive at something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added أَ at the start to mean 'I', and changed the vowels to show it's happening now (present tense) instead of already happened (past tense)
Added vowels to connect the root letters and form a singular noun
Regular plural form - adds alif at start and changes middle vowels to make many 'means/ways'
Adding definite article to make the noun definite

steps.m: the means, the ways (object of أَبْلُغُ - I may reach)
steps.n: Changed to show this word is being acted upon by the verb 'I may reach' (أَبْلُغُ)

steps.m: a means or way (something that connects)
steps.n: Added vowels to make a noun meaning the thing that connects or creates a path to something
Changed to plural by adding أَ at start and ا after ب. This is a common plural pattern that means 'multiple ways or means'.
Changed to show this word is being acted upon by the verb 'so I may look at' (فَأَطَّلِعَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The verb 'to rise up' became a noun meaning 'sky' by adding ء at the end
Changes sky/heaven into plural by adding -wat at the end and putting a و (waw) before it
Adding ال makes it specific/definite
The word 'heavens' gets a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end to show it belongs to or is connected to another word (أَسْبٰبَ) in the sentence

steps.m: to look up at/rise up to see
he looked up/rose up to see
steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and doubled ط to show doing something thoroughly
Changed to first person present tense with أ prefix

steps.m: so that I may look closely (at إِلٰهِ مُوسَىٰ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is the purpose or result of the previous action. This word connects to 'the god of Moses' (إِلٰهِ مُوسَىٰ) in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. It's one of the core particles in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
Added an alif maksura (ىٰ) with a small alif above it to show proper pronunciation. This connects to the word إِلٰهِ (god) that follows it.
Added a small vertical line (ٓ) above to indicate slight elongation in pronunciation when connecting to the next word.
Changed initial hamza to kasra hamza and added vowel marks to create the noun form meaning deity
Added a small alif (dagger alif) after the second letter to show the long 'aa' sound
Added kasra at the end to show this is possessed by or connected to something (in this case, connected to Moses)

steps.m: Moses (the prophet mentioned in إِلٰهِ مُوسَىٰ)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out of water'. In this ayah, it refers to Prophet Moses who Pharaoh is speaking about. The long alif (ىٰ) at the end is a special way of writing foreign names in Arabic.
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English
Added ى at the end to show this is about me (I/my)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before, like saying 'and'
Basic verb form
Changed root letters into past tense verb 'he thought' by adding vowels and doubling middle letter
Changes to first person present tense by adding أَ at start and changing vowels
Adds لَ to emphasize certainty of thinking
Adds هُ at end to show who is being thought about
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes from 'he lied' to 'liar' by adding an alif and changing middle vowel - makes a noun meaning 'someone who does the action'

steps.m: a liar (object of أَظُنُّهُ - I think him)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is being acted upon by the verb أَظُنُّ (I think/believe). Pharaoh is saying he thinks Moses is a liar.
Starting with the basic demonstrative word 'this'. The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) but means the same thing.
Added ك at the beginning to show similarity or comparison
Added ل to strengthen the meaning of 'like/similar to'
Added ك at the end to complete the expression meaning 'in this way' or 'likewise'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Basic past tense form
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels to show the action was done to something rather than by something
This is the base form of the proper noun for the ruler of ancient Egypt. The vowel marks help with pronunciation.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by something
Added لِ at the beginning to show direction or purpose - like saying 'to Pharaoh' or 'for Pharaoh'
Added damma on first letter to turn the action into a noun meaning the harmful thing itself

steps.m: evil (subject of زُيِّنَ - was made attractive)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this word is doing the action. In this verse, this evil was made to look attractive to Pharaoh.
Basic past tense meaning 'he did/performed'
Adding the basic vowel pattern to make it a complete past tense verb
Added هِ (his) at the end to show the deed belongs to him. The word is connected to سُوٓءُ (evil) to mean 'evil of his deed'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Converting to passive voice pattern
Doubling the middle letter intensifies the meaning

steps.m: and was forcefully blocked/prevented (from the path mentioned after)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this word to the previous phrase. This word connects to عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (from the path) that comes after it.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows movement or turning away from something

steps.m: from/away from (connects to السَّبِيلِ - the path)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) at the end to connect smoothly with the next word السَّبِيلِ (the path). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to travel a path or way

steps.n: The ي and vowels change it from a verb into a noun meaning 'path'
Added ٱل to specify this particular path, and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ٱل

steps.m: from/of the path (object of عَنِ meaning 'away from')
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it follows عَنِ which means 'away from' in this ayah
This is a particle that can mean either 'what' (asking about something) or 'not' (negating something). Here it means 'not' because it's negating 'Pharaoh's plot' (كَيْدُ فِرْعَوْنَ)
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This و means 'and'.

steps.m: to plot or make secret plans
steps.n: This is the basic meaning showing the idea of making secret plans to achieve something
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and turn the action into a thing - like changing 'to plot' into 'a plot'
Added ُ at the end to show this is the main thing doing something in the sentence. It belongs to Pharaoh, showing it's 'Pharaoh's plot'.
This is the base form of the proper noun title used for ancient Egyptian rulers

steps.m: Pharaoh (object of زُيِّنَ - was made attractive to)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb زُيِّنَ (was made attractive to)
This is an exception particle that excludes what comes after it. It's a frozen form that doesn't undergo morphological derivation.
This word introduces an exception, showing that Pharaoh's plot will end in nothing but ruin. It connects what comes before it (Pharaoh's plot) with what comes after it (in ruin).
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects to the word تَبَابٍ (ruin/loss) to show being 'in ruin/loss'.
Basic vowel marks (fatha) added to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he destroyed'
Added alif to make it into a noun meaning the result of destruction
Added tanwin kasra to show this describes a state or condition
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas

steps.m: and he said (introducing what the believer said to his people)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This introduces the words of the believer to his people.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which', used to connect descriptions to people or things

steps.m: the one who (connects to ءَامَنَ - the one who believed)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it more specific, like changing 'who' to 'the one who'

steps.m: the one who (connects to ءَامَنَ - the one who believed)
steps.n: The ال changes to ٱلّ with a hamza wasl and shadda for easier pronunciation

steps.m: the one who (connects to ءَامَنَ - the one who believed)
steps.n: The ي changes to ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top for proper Quranic writing

steps.m: to feel safe and trust in something

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

Changed the first letter to hamza with madd (ءَا) and added alif after it. This changes the meaning to actively putting trust in something (believing). This person is the one who says 'O my people' (يٰقَوْمِ) in this verse.

steps.m: to stand firm, establish, rise up

steps.n: Added fatha and sukun to form the noun. The meaning shifts from the action of standing to refer to a group that stands together as a community.

steps.m: O my people! (being called by the believer)
steps.n: Added يٰ at start to call out to the people, and ِ at end to show the speaker is claiming them as their people. This word is being spoken by the believer mentioned in the previous part of the verse (ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَ).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added Form VIII pattern showing deliberate, thoughtful following
Changed from 'he followed' to 'you follow!' by changing the middle vowel and ending - makes it a command to one person
Added و to make it plural (addressing many people)
Added نِ at the end to mean 'follow me' instead of just 'follow'

steps.m: to guide or show the way

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif prefix and changing vowel makes it a present tense verb meaning 'I guide'

steps.m: I guide you all (object of أَهْدِكُمْ in اتَّبِعُونِ أَهْدِكُمْ)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who is being guided. This connects to the previous command 'follow me' (اتَّبِعُونِ), completing the thought 'follow me, I will guide you all'
Adds ي to make it a specific type of path/way
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb أَهْدِكُمْ (I will guide you)
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed the verb into a noun showing the result of guidance
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ر because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after سَبِيلَ (path) in the phrase 'path of right guidance'

steps.m: to stand firm, establish, rise up

steps.n: Root meaning 'stand' becomes 'people' - like a group standing together. Made into a noun by adding fatha and sukun marks
Adding kasra at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else
Added يٰ at the beginning to make it a call or address to people
Basic emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'indeed' in English
Adding ما at the end changes 'indeed' to 'indeed only', restricting what comes after to just one meaning
This is the basic demonstrative word used to point to something nearby
The alif above ه (called an overbar) helps with pronunciation and shows this is for nearby things
Added kasra to help connect with the next letter
Added ه with kasra at the end to make it feminine, showing we're pointing to a feminine thing (in this case, referring to ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ - 'the life')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he lived' (verb) to 'life' (noun) by adding و and ة at the end - like changing 'live' to 'life' in English
Added ٱلْ to specify this particular life (referring to worldly life in this context)

steps.m: the life (subject of this part of the ayah)
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) to show this word is the subject being described
Root meaning 'near/close' becomes a word meaning 'worldly life' - like something closer/nearer to us than the afterlife
Adds 'the' (ال) to make it specific, like changing 'worldly life' to 'the worldly life'
Adds emphasis to show this is specifically about worldly life, as opposed to the afterlife mentioned later in the ayah (ٱلْءَاخِرَةَ)
Connected the root letters م-ت-ع with short vowels (fatha) to make the noun 'enjoyment'
Added a long 'aa' sound after ت to make it a noun meaning provision or enjoyment

steps.m: a temporary enjoyment (subject of this ayah)
steps.n: The double-damma ending shows this is the subject being described in this part of the verse
This is an emphatic particle that makes what follows more certain and adds emphasis. It's like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English.

steps.m: and indeed (connects this emphatic statement to the previous one)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this statement to the previous one, showing that both parts are related. In this ayah, it connects the statement about the afterlife to the previous statement about worldly life.
Added alif after first letter to show who/what does the action. This creates the active participle pattern فَاعِل.
Added feminine ending ة to specify this refers to the afterlife/next life specifically

Added ٱلْ to make it definite ('the'), and َ at the end shows it's the object of إِنَّ (indeed/verily). This word is part of the phrase إِنَّ ٱلْآخِرَةَ هِىَ دَارُ ٱلْقَرَارِ meaning 'indeed the Hereafter is the eternal home'
This is the base form of the feminine pronoun
Added ى and َ to make it a complete independent pronoun. This word is the subject of دَارُ (home/abode) in this ayah, showing that the afterlife (feminine) is the permanent home.

steps.m: to move in a circle or stay in one place

steps.n: Added vowels to the root letters to make a past tense verb meaning 'he stayed'
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: to settle or remain in one place

steps.n: Changed from an action to a noun meaning a place where something settles permanently. This word is owned by دَارُ (abode) in the phrase دَارُ ٱلْقَرَارِ (abode of permanence).
Added 'ال' (the) at the beginning and a kasra (i-sound) at the end because it's connected to دَارُ (abode)
This is a special word that means 'whoever' and introduces a condition. In this ayah, it starts two parallel statements: 'whoever does evil...' and 'whoever does good...'
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Connected the root letters with basic vowels to form the noun meaning badness
The middle letter is doubled to show intensity, and و changes to ي
Added ة to make it refer to one specific instance of badness - a bad deed

steps.m: a bad deed (object of عَمِلَ - did)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object of the verb عَمِلَ (did/performed)
This is the basic negative particle that negates what comes after it

steps.m: then not (negates يُجْزَىٰٓ - will be recompensed)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. Here it connects to يُجْزَىٰٓ (will be recompensed) to say 'then they will not be recompensed'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added يُ at start to show present tense and changed vowels to show passive voice (something being done to someone)
Added alif maksura (ٰ) to show the long 'aa' sound at the end
Added ى (alif maqsura) to properly write the long 'aa' sound at end of word
This is a basic particle that shows an exception - it means something is left out of what was mentioned before
Added kasra (ِ) on first letter and shadda (ّ) on second letter to show how to pronounce it correctly

steps.m: except (what comes after this word is excluded from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) and alif (ا) to make the complete word meaning 'except'. This word shows that something is being excluded from what was mentioned before.

steps.m: to be similar or equal to something

steps.n: This form creates a noun meaning 'likeness' or 'similarity'. In this ayah, it refers to being equal to the evil deed (سَيِّئَةً) mentioned earlier.
Added feminine singular pronoun suffix هَا which refers back to the evil deed (سَيِّئَةً). The َ (fatha) before هَا is needed to connect it smoothly.
This is a word used to refer to people in a general way, like saying 'whoever' or 'anyone who' in English
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This introduces a new case after the previous one, connecting to the verb عَمِلَ (does/acts)
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun describing someone who has this quality

steps.m: a righteous deed (object of عَمِلَ - did/performed)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon. This word tells us what was done - a righteous deed - in relation to the verb عَمِلَ (did/performed).
This is a primitive preposition that has no root or derivational steps. It's one of the foundational particles in Arabic.
Added shadda to emphasize the م. This word connects 'male' (ذَكَرٍ) back to 'whoever does' (عَمِلَ), showing that the doer could be from among males.

steps.m: to remember, to mention, male (especially human/animal)
male (contrasting with أُنثَىٰ/female in this ayah)
steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make the noun 'male', which pairs with 'female' in this ayah

steps.m: a male (being acted upon in this ayah)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite (any male) and is being acted upon in the sentence
This is a connecting word that gives a choice between male (ذَكَرٍ) or female (أُنثَىٰ). It works like 'or' in English when listing options.
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'female'
Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) to show elongation. This word appears in contrast to ذَكَرٍ (male) in this ayah.
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it's the subject of مُؤْمِنٌ (believer), showing who is being described as a believer.
Added و with fatha (وَ) at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it, showing this is an additional description of the person mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this is a past tense verb
Changed the word to show who does the action by adding مُ at the start and changing the vowels. The ؤ appears because أ changes when it comes after ُ.

steps.m: a believer (describing someone in the ayah)
steps.n: Added the ٌ mark to show this is describing someone in general terms. In this ayah, it's describing someone who does good deeds.
Starting with the basic demonstrative pronoun and adding vowel marks to show it's plural
Adding ئِ to show these are far away (not close by)
Adding كَ at the end to make the word stronger and clearer when pointing to things far away

steps.m: so/then those (who do good deeds) (subject of يَدْخُلُونَ)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning to show these people are the result of what was mentioned before. This word connects to يَدْخُلُونَ (they enter) - showing who will enter Paradise.
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense
Added ونَ at the end to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: to cover or conceal with dense growth

steps.n: Adding ة makes it refer to a specific place characterized by dense covering (garden)
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific paradise mentioned in the Quran (object of يَدْخُلُونَ - they enter)
The fatha shows Paradise is what's being entered (object of يَدْخُلُونَ - they enter)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he provided' to 'he is being provided' by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to show someone is receiving the action
Added ون to show multiple people are receiving the provision. This refers to the believers (مُؤْمِنٌ) mentioned earlier who will enter Paradise.
Combination of في (in) with ها (it/her)
Basic noun form indicating 'other than' or 'without'. It is in the genitive case because it is possessed by the word حِسَاب (account/reckoning), forming the phrase 'without account'.
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or connected to حِسَاب (reckoning/account)
Added بِ at the beginning to show this describes how something is done - in this case, receiving provision without any reckoning
Changed from an action to a noun - from the act of counting to the process of calculation itself
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite and follows غَيْرِ (without) in the phrase بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (without any reckoning)
Root meaning 'stand' became a noun meaning 'people' by adding vowels - like a group that stands together
Added يٰ at the beginning to call out to people directly

steps.m: And O my people! (speaking before أَدْعُوكُمْ - I call you)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at start and ِ at end to show possession (my)

steps.m: what (asking about the reason for أَدْعُوكُمْ)
steps.n: This is a question word that asks 'what' or 'why'. In this ayah, it's asking about the reason, like saying 'What is wrong with me that...' or 'Why is it that...'

steps.m: what (asking about the reason for أَدْعُوكُمْ)
steps.n: Added fatha mark on م to show the correct pronunciation
Starting with the basic preposition لِ (to/for) and adding ى (me) to show who it's for
Added elongation mark (ٓ) to show this word is connected to أَدْعُوكُمْ (I call you) in this ayah
The fatha vowels show this is a basic past tense verb meaning 'he did something'
Adding أ at start makes it 'I' form, vowel change shows it's present tense

steps.m: I call/invite you all (object of verb)
steps.n: Adds كُمْ at the end to show who is being called. This كُمْ means 'you all' and shows that multiple people are being addressed.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'I call you' (أَدْعُوكُمْ) with 'salvation' (ٱلنَّجَوٰةِ), showing what they are being called to.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from an action (to save) to the concept of salvation itself
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda
The word got a kasra (i) sound at the end because it comes after إِلَى (to). The long 'a' sound is written differently but pronounced the same.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense. The final و shows it's an ongoing action.
The doubled ن (نّ) adds emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'definitely'

steps.m: and you all call me (while calling you to النَّجَوٰةِ/salvation)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at start to connect sentences. Changed نّ to نِى which means 'me' - showing they are calling to me/Allah

This is a basic preposition that shows movement or direction towards something. In this ayah, it connects 'I call you' (أَدْعُوكُمْ) with 'salvation' (النَّجَوٰةِ) to mean 'I call you towards salvation'
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'fire'
Added ٱل (al) at the beginning to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because it follows the word إِلَى (to/towards) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the letters. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense. The ا changes back to و.
Added ن to show multiple people are doing the action
Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is being called

steps.m: to reject, deny, or cover up truth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added أَ at start to make it 'I' form of the verb

steps.m: so that I reject/deny (connected to تَدْعُونَنِى)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show purpose - this explains why they are being called/invited (تَدْعُونَنِى)
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one true God, not just any deity
Added fatha on the shadda for proper pronunciation
Changed initial alif to wasl form since it connects with previous letter

steps.m: by/with Allah (object of أَكْفُرَ - to disbelieve)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'by/with' and kasra at end because it's the object of the verb أَكْفُرَ (to disbelieve)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive voice with hamza prefix and vowel pattern change to show something was done to the subject

steps.m: and was made to associate partners (object of تَدْعُونَنِى/you call me to)
steps.n: Added و to connect this clause with the previous one. This whole phrase is what they are calling him to do.
Added kasra to show how the letter is pronounced
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'with it' referring back to the word عِلْمٌ (knowledge) mentioned later in the ayah
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

This is a basic particle that can mean different things based on context. Here it's used as a relative pronoun meaning 'that which' because it refers to whatever false deities they're calling him to worship.
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
This is a special verb that only comes in past tense form but gives present meaning. It's used to negate things.
Adding the basic vowel pattern to make it a usable verb
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is connecting to something

steps.m: for me/to me (connected to تَدْعُونَنِى - you call me)
steps.n: Added ى to show this means 'me'. This word connects to تَدْعُونَنِى (you call me) to show what you're calling me to do.
Added kasra (ِ) to show how the letter is pronounced
Added هِ which means 'him/it' to show what/who the word 'with' connects to. In this ayah, it refers back to Allah mentioned earlier.
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Root meaning 'know' becomes the noun 'knowledge' by adding the vowel marks kasra (ِ) and sukoon (ْ)
Added double damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence
Added vowel marks to the basic pronoun 'I'. This word is the subject of the verb أَدْعُوكُمْ (I call you) in this ayah.
Added the conjunction 'and' at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before.
Added a small alif (۠) above the final alif. This is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Added basic past tense vowels. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added أ at start to make it first person ('I'). Changed middle vowel to ُ for present tense.

steps.m: I call/invite you all (object of أَدْعُو)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being called/invited (you all)
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'I call you' (أَدْعُوكُمْ) with 'the Mighty' (ٱلْعَزِيزِ), showing that the calling is directed towards Allah.
Added ي between the last two letters and vowels to show this describes someone or something that has a lot of strength or power
Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end because it comes after إِلَى (to) in the ayah. This is one of Allah's names showing His absolute might and power.
Added vowels to connect the letters, making it a past tense verb
The doubled letter ف with ّ and the added ا make this describe someone who forgives again and again

steps.m: The Most Forgiving (as a name of Allah)
steps.n: Adding ال makes this a title referring specifically to Allah, one of His divine names

steps.m: The Most Forgiving (as the object after إِلَى)
steps.n: The ِ shows this word comes after إِلَى (meaning 'to') in the phrase 'calling you to The Most Forgiving'
Basic negative particle that makes statements negative. In this ayah, it negates دَعْوَةٌ (call/invocation), showing there is no call/invocation.
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: to cut off, to establish decisively
it became decisively established (used with لا to mean 'undoubtedly')
steps.n: When combined with لا before it, لا جَرَمَ becomes a fixed expression meaning 'undoubtedly/certainly'. The original meaning of 'cutting off/establishing decisively' evolved to express absolute certainty.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain

steps.m: that which, indeed what (connects to تَدْعُونَنِىٓ - you call me to)
steps.n: Adding ما changes this from just 'indeed' to 'that which' or 'indeed what'. This compound introduces what is being called to in the next word تَدْعُونَنِىٓ (you call me to).

steps.m: to call out to or invite someone

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make the basic past tense form. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a special letter.
Changed to present tense with ت for 'you' and different vowels to show ongoing action
Added نَ to show multiple people are doing the action
Added نِي at the end to show 'me' is receiving the action
Added ى with a special mark to stretch out the sound at the end
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
The ى changes to ي with sukun (ْ) to prepare for connecting with the pronoun. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
The هِ (meaning 'him') is added at the end. This shows who or what is being referred to.
This is a special negative verb that means 'is not'. In this ayah, it negates what comes after it, meaning that what they are calling to has no power.
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced
Added هُ at the end to show who the word 'for' refers to (him)
Added a small و mark (ۥ) after هُ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of calling
Added tanween dammah to show this is the subject of لَيْسَ (is not) in this ayah
Basic preposition showing location or being inside something

steps.m: in (connects فِى الدُّنْيَا with لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَعْوَةٌ)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to complete the word. In this ayah, it connects 'no call/supplication' with 'in this world', showing where there is no response to the false calls.
Root meaning 'near' became a noun by adding special pattern and alif at end to mean 'nearest/closest life'
Added ال to make it specific - 'the worldly life' instead of just any worldly thing
Added a doubling mark (shadda) on the د to make the sound stronger
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'no'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

This is a basic preposition that shows location or presence within something. Here it connects 'call/invitation' (دَعْوَةٌ) with 'the worldly life' (ٱلدُّنْيَا) to mean 'call/invitation in the worldly life'

steps.m: to come after or be delayed

steps.n: Adding آ at start makes it mean 'more/most after' = 'last'
Added ة to create a special feminine noun referring to the afterlife
Added ال (the) at the beginning and kasra at the end to show this word is connected to في from the previous word
This is a particle that emphasizes what comes after it and connects it to what came before. It shows that something is definitely true.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this statement with the previous one. This connects to the word مَرَدَّنَآ (our return) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show doubled letter
Added مَ at beginning to show this is a place where the action happens
Added نَا at the end to show it belongs to us, with آ showing elongated sound
Basic preposition indicating direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) to show 'to Allah'.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Changed from the general word for 'a god' to the special name 'Allah' by adding ال and joining letters together
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to 'our return' in the phrase 'our return is to Allah'
This is a special particle that emphasizes what comes after it and connects it to what came before. It's like saying 'I confirm that...' in English.
Added و at the beginning to connect this statement to the previous one, like adding 'and' in English
The مُ prefix and ِ vowel work together to create a noun describing someone who does this action habitually

steps.m: those who go beyond limits (object of أَنَّ)
steps.n: The ين ending makes it plural (more than two). This word is in the accusative case because it comes after the particle أنّ which puts its noun in accusative case.
ٱلْ at the start makes it specific/definite (like 'the' in English)
This is the basic form of the pronoun that refers to a group of males or a mixed group. In this ayah, it connects to أَصْحٰبُ (companions/people) to show who the companions are.
Added damma (ُ) on ه and sukun (ْ) on م to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: to be close to or accompany someone

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to accompany'
Changed from 'he accompanied' to 'companions' by using the plural pattern أَفْعَال - like how we make 'friend' into 'friends' in English
Changed regular alif to overbar alif (alif khanjariyah) - this is just a writing style change that doesn't affect meaning
Changed the middle letter و to ا and added fatha to make the noun for 'fire'
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda

steps.m: of the fire (possessed by أَصْحٰبُ - companions/people)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this word belongs to أَصْحٰبُ (companions/people) in the phrase 'companions of the fire'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Dropped first vowel and changed middle vowel to 'u' to make present tense
Added تَ prefix to show second person (you)
Added ونَ to make it plural (you all)
Added سَ to show future tense
Added فَ to connect with previous statement, meaning 'then/so'
This is a non-derived particle used for questions or relative clauses. Not derived from a root.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Changed alif to alif madd (آ) to show long vowel sound. This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense form
The root قَوَلَ changes its vowels to قُولُ to show it's happening now instead of in the past. The middle و blends with the vowels around it.

steps.m: I say (to لَكُمْ - to you all)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action. This word connects to لَكُمْ (to you all) later in the ayah.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added كُمْ (you all) to show who the action is directed to. This word is receiving the action of أَقُولُ (I say) in this ayah.

steps.m: to hand over, to give full authority

steps.n: Added shadda to show complete/intensive action of handing over authority
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding أُ at start, and changed from past to present tense

steps.m: and I completely hand over (connected to فَسَتَذْكُرُونَ - you will remember)
steps.n: Added conjunction و to connect this action with the previous statement 'you will remember'

steps.m: to command, order, or deal with a matter

steps.n: Changed from a basic root meaning 'to command' into a noun meaning 'matter/command' by making the middle letter silent (with sukun)
Added ي with kasra to show possession. This word is the object of أُفَوِّضُ (I entrust) in this ayah.
Added alif extension mark (ٓ) after ى for Quranic writing style. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a basic word that shows direction or connection. Here it connects 'my affair' to 'Allah'.
Added vowel marks to show exactly how to pronounce the word - kasra (ِ) under إ and fatha (َ) over ل
Changed the verb concept into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the') and the shadda emphasizes the ل. The kasra at the end shows this connects to what comes after it in the sentence.
Adding shadda makes this word emphasize what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that Allah is truly All-Seeing of His servants.
Changed the verb concept into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the') and the doubled ل shows emphasis. The َ at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changes to intensive form showing this is a permanent, complete quality (used as an attribute of Allah in this ayah)
The tanween (double damma) shows this is a complete standalone description
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes from singular 'worshipper' to plural form for multiple worshippers
Adds 'the' and genitive case ending because it comes after the preposition بِ
Adds preposition بِ showing how Allah sees or deals with His servants
Changed root letters into past tense verb by adding vowels (fatha) and alif at end
Added فَ at start to mean 'and then/so'. This connects it to what happened before. Allah (اللَّهُ) is doing the protecting.

steps.m: and He protected him (object is the believer mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) at the end to show who was protected. This refers back to the believing man mentioned in the story.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added the u-vowel at the end to show this is the subject
Added 'the' at the start to make it specific/definite

steps.m: to be harmful or cause trouble

steps.n: Root letters combined with vowels to make a noun meaning 'badness'
Changed the middle letter to ي with shadda to show intensity
Added feminine plural ending ات to show multiple evil deeds
Changed ending to kasra (-i) because it's receiving the action of being protected from

steps.m: that which (connects to مَكَرُوا - they plotted)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb مَكَرُوا (they plotted) after it. It means 'that which they plotted' in this context.

steps.m: to plan secretly (usually with bad intentions)

steps.n: Added fatha (َ) after each letter to make the basic past tense verb. This word is connected to فَوَقَىٰهُ (so Allah protected him) in the ayah, showing what the person did that required protection.
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together. The small circle (۟) helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.
The middle letter ي changes to ا and we add fathas to show this happened in the past

steps.m: and surrounded/overtook completely (connected to فَوَقَىٰهُ - so He protected him)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect with how Allah protected him in the previous phrase, showing the contrast between protection and punishment
The أ and و combine to form آ (a long alif)
Added kasra to show possession/connection
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with/by'. This word connects to فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh) to mean 'with/by the family of Pharaoh'

steps.m: Pharaoh (the title of ancient Egyptian kings)
steps.n: This is a proper noun (name) that doesn't come from a root. It is a borrowed word from ancient Egyptian 'per-aa' meaning 'great house'

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object being affected by حَاقَ - surrounded)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Pharaoh is the object being affected by the verb حَاقَ (surrounded/encompassed) in this ayah
Changed from a root meaning 'to harm' into a noun meaning 'evil' by adding damma (u sound) in the middle
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in حَاقَ (surrounded/encompassed)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun by adding alif after the second root letter
Added ال to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because it's the object of the verb حَاقَ (befell/surrounded). Also changed the alif to wasl form since it's the standard writing.
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'fire'
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and doubled the ن (shown by ّ) because it comes after ٱلْ
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject being talked about in the sentence. The fire is what they are exposed to (يُعْرَضُونَ) in the morning and evening.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to passive form to show the action is being done to someone rather than by someone

steps.m: they are being presented (to النَّارُ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are being presented. In this ayah, they are being presented to the Fire (النَّارُ)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting with the pronoun

steps.m: upon it/her (referring to النَّارُ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to the Fire (النَّارُ) mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: to go or come in early morning

steps.n: Changes from basic root to noun showing the act of going in morning

steps.m: in the early morning (describing when they are presented to the fire)
steps.n: Adding tanween fatHa (ًا) shows this word describes when the action happens. It connects to يُعْرَضُونَ (they are presented) in the ayah.
The shadda at the end makes it mean 'time of evening'

steps.m: in the evening (time when they are shown to the fire)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this describes when something happens. This word tells us when they are shown to the fire (يُعْرَضُونَ).

steps.m: and in the evening (paired with غُدُوًّا meaning morning)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect it with غُدُوًّا (morning) mentioned before, showing these are the two times they are shown to the fire
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'day'
Adding fatha at the end shows this word tells us when something happens. Here it's telling us when the Hour/Day of Judgment (ٱلسَّاعَةُ) will come (تَقُومُ)
Added وَ at the start to connect this to what came before, showing this is another time when something happens
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The middle ا returns to و.
Changes from 'he' to 'you/she' by adding ت at start

steps.m: to move or measure in time
steps.n: Connected the root letters with basic vowels
The root letters س-و-ع become the word 'hour/time' by adding ا in the middle and ة at the end to make it a feminine noun

steps.m: The Hour (referring to the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to the specific Hour of Judgment, and causes the س to get a shadda
Adding damma shows this is the subject doing the standing/coming in تَقُومُ
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added أَ at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action
Changed from past tense 'he made enter' to a command 'make enter!' by changing middle vowel to 'i' and removing last vowel

steps.m: make (all of you) enter! (command to group)
steps.n: Added وا to make it a command to multiple people
This root relates to being first or leading, which connects to the concept of family/followers who come after a leader
Basic past tense verb form with vowels added to show 'he' did the action
These are actually two different words - not a transformation. آل means 'family/followers' while أول means 'first/to lead'. They just look similar but have different roots and meanings.
This is a proper noun (title) borrowed from ancient Egyptian that doesn't come from an Arabic root
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the command 'enter' (أَدْخِلُوٓا۟)
Connected the root letters and added the shadda to show the doubled letter
