Adding أَ at the start makes this word mean 'most' or 'more' of something. Here it's describing how intense the punishment is.
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the result of punishment. The ا after ذ makes it stronger, showing this is intense or severe punishment.

steps.m: the punishment (that will be given to آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to a specific punishment - in this case, the punishment mentioned for Pharaoh's people

steps.m: of the punishment (describing أَشَدَّ - the severest)
steps.n: The ِ ending shows this word describes أَشَدَّ (severest), telling us what is most severe - the punishment
This is a time word that shows something happened in the past. In this ayah, it refers to when the people argue in the Fire.

steps.m: and when (referring to when the people argue in the Fire)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to what came before it. This و is like saying 'and' in English.
Base root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Added تَ prefix to show people arguing with each other

steps.m: they are arguing with each other
steps.n: Added يَ prefix to show the action is happening now

steps.m: they are arguing with each other (in النَّارِ)
steps.n: Added ون ending to show multiple people doing the action. The word appears in the context of people arguing in the Fire (النَّارِ)
Basic preposition showing location inside something. In this ayah, it shows the people are inside the Fire of Hell while arguing.
Base root meaning
Changes from root to noun meaning fire
Added ال to make it specific and shadda on ن because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from/of) which requires this ending
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form. The middle و changes to ا which is common with this type of word.
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The middle ا returns to original و.

steps.m: and then he says (after the arguing mentioned in وَإِذْ يَتَحَآجُّونَ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this saying happens as a result of the arguing mentioned before
Base root meaning
Shows the process of becoming weak or feeble
Changed pattern to make adjective describing someone who is weak
Changed to plural form for people who are weak
Added 'the' to specify these particular weak ones
The weak ones - plural form with a special ending that shows they are speaking to someone
Starting with the basic demonstrative word that points to something
Adding ال and ي changes it from pointing at something to describing someone or something
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people instead of just one

steps.m: to those who (who are addressed by ٱلضُّعَفٰٓؤُا۟)
steps.n: Adding لِ at the beginning shows direction or purpose. The ل combines with ال to form لِلّ with a shadda. This word is being spoken by ٱلضُّعَفٰٓؤُا۟ (the weak ones) to address others.
Connected root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Form X verb indicating reflexive/seeking meaning - considering oneself to be great
Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people acting arrogantly
Added helping ا to make it easier to pronounce the word starting with س
This is an emphasis particle that makes statements stronger. The shadda shows the doubled ن.
Added ا at the end to mean 'we'. This word connects to كُنَّا (we were) in the next part of the ayah.
Base root meaning
Adding vowel marks creates basic past tense verb meaning 'he was'
The middle weak letter و drops out and first vowel changes to damma because this is a hollow verb (has و in middle)
Added fatha to show the basic preposition form

steps.m: for you all (being addressed by الضُّعَفٰٓؤُا۟)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this is addressing multiple people. In this ayah, the weak ones (الضُّعَفٰٓؤُا۟) are speaking to their former leaders.
Base root meaning
Adding vowel marks creates basic past tense verb meaning 'he followed'
Changed from 'he followed' (a verb) to 'followers' (a noun) by adding tanween alif at the end, showing these people are being described as followers
This is a question word that expects yes or no answer

steps.m: so is/are (question to disbelievers in ٱلنَّارِ)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this question to the previous statement, showing it's a result or consequence. The disbelievers in hellfire are being asked this question.
This is the basic part of the pronoun that means 'you'
These are actually two different words - أَنْ means 'that' while أَنْتُ means 'you'
Added م to make it refer to multiple people. This pronoun is the subject of مُّغْنُونَ (able to help/avail) in this ayah.
Connected root letters with fatha vowels to make basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who frees from need (subject of أَنتُم)
steps.n: Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show someone who does this action. This word is part of أَنتُم (you all) in the ayah.

steps.m: ones who free from need (plural, subject of أَنتُم)
steps.n: Added masculine plural ending to indicate multiple helpers

steps.m: ones who definitely/surely free from need
steps.n: Adding shadda (double letter) makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
This is a basic preposition showing separation or distance. It's used with verbs to show moving away from something.

steps.m: away from us (object of مُّغْنُونَ - those who can help)
steps.n: Added نا (meaning 'us') to show who is being moved away from. The ن at the end of عَن combines with the ن from نا to make one stronger ن (shown by ّ). This word is connected to مُّغْنُونَ (those who can help) in the ayah.

steps.m: to establish, set up, or assign a share
steps.n: Base root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changes verb 'establish' into noun meaning 'portion/share'
Added tanween (two small lines) at the end to show this is receiving the action - it's the portion being talked about
This is a preposition showing source or origin, used here to show what they want to be saved from
Added shadda for emphasis because it follows another word ending in a vowel
Added fatha at the end because it's connected to the next word النَّارِ (the Fire). Together they mean 'from the Fire'
Changed the root letters to make a noun meaning 'fire' - something that gives light and burns
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being used after a preposition

steps.m: the intense fire (possessed by/connected to something)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) to show this is talking about a specific fire, not just any fire
Added fathas (َ) after each letter to show this happened in the past and was done by 'he'
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا, which is common for hollow verbs
This is the basic form used to point to someone or something that does an action
Added ال with shadda to make it specific and connect it to what comes after

steps.m: those who (plural, subject of قَالَ - said)
steps.n: Changed to plural form by adding ن at the end. This word refers to a group who are speaking in this ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show basic past tense meaning
The prefix است shows someone trying to act bigger than they really are - like pretending to be more important
Changed initial alif to wasl form and added plural marker. This word is the subject of قَالَ (said) in this ayah.
Added kasra under إ and shadda on ن to make the emphatic particle that starts statements

steps.m: indeed we (speaking to those who were arrogant)
steps.n: Added نا at the end in the form of ا to show 'we' are the subject being emphasized. This is part of a conversation between people in the Fire.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added double damma at the end to show this is the main subject doing the action in the sentence.
Added kasra vowel mark to show the basic preposition meaning
Added ها at the end to show this refers to something feminine (in this case, referring back to being in the Fire)
Changed the alif to a long alif (آ) which is just a writing variation that doesn't change the meaning
This is the basic form of the particle that connects parts of sentences
Adding the shadda changes this from a basic connecting word to one that adds strong emphasis. Here it emphasizes the statement about Allah (اللَّهَ) that follows.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added shadda on ل to show emphasis, fatha at end because it's the object of إِنَّ (indeed), and changed initial alif to wasl form
Basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to the verb حَكَمَ (has judged) that follows it

steps.m: to judge between right and wrong

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past. In this ayah, Allah is the one who judged between all the people.
Added vowel marks to create the preposition form. This word connects two things by showing the space that separates them.
Added fatha at the end because this word is connecting to 'the servants' (ٱلْعِبَادِ) that comes after it in the ayah
Connected the root letters together to form the basic noun for one who serves
Changes singular 'servant' into plural form for multiple servants
Added ال to make it specific servants being discussed
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word بَيْنَ (between) in the ayah
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha marks
Changed to common past tense form by adding alif

steps.m: and he said (to خَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ - the keepers of Hell)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before. This word shows who is speaking (the people in the Fire) and who they are speaking to (the keepers of Hell).
This is a relative pronoun used to describe a group of people
This is a basic preposition showing location or position
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly
Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation - it means the same thing
The و (weak letter) changes to ا to make the noun for 'fire' from the root meaning 'to give light/heat'
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is being described by another word
Adding ٱل at the start makes it specific - not just any fire, but a particular fire (in this case, the fire of Hell)

steps.m: of the intense fire (possessed by جَهَنَّمَ/Hell)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this fire belongs to Hell (جَهَنَّمَ) mentioned right after it in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to form the active participle (doer of the action)
Adding ة at the end makes this a broken plural form meaning 'guardians'
Added لِ ('to/for') at the start and changed the ending to show it connects to Hell (جَهَنَّمَ)
This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew 'Gehinnom', referring to the place of punishment in the afterlife. Since it's a borrowed word, it doesn't come from an Arabic root.

steps.m: Hell (the object being addressed in this ayah)
steps.n: Added vowel marks and shadda to show proper pronunciation. The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence - the people in the Fire are speaking to the keepers of Hell.

steps.m: to call out to someone or invite them

steps.n: Changed root letters into command form by adding a stop mark (sukun) and 'oo' sound (damma) - like saying 'Stop and call!'
Added the special alif that helps start command words

steps.m: all of you call! (command to a group)
steps.n: Added the plural marker to show this command is for many people

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop, raise

steps.n: Added fatha and shadda to create the noun meaning 'lord' or 'master' - someone who takes care of others
Adding the possessive pronoun suffix كم to indicate 'your (plural)'
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show doing the action strongly
Added يُ at the beginning to show it's happening now, and changed the middle vowel to kasra
Added sukun at the end because this word is part of a request to lighten punishment

steps.m: from us (addressing خَزَنَةِ جَهَنَّمَ - the keepers of Hell)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show this means 'us'. The shadda (ّ) appears because when you add نا to عَنْ, the ن from عَنْ combines with the ن from نا. The people in the Fire are asking the keepers of Hell to reduce their punishment.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و takes a sukun because it's a weak letter.
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence. It's what they want lightened - 'a day' of punishment.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'. Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation.
Added shadda for emphasis and to show strong connection with the following word العَذَابِ (the punishment).
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
The verb 'he punished' (عَذَبَ) becomes the noun 'punishment' (عَذَاب) by changing the middle letter to a long ā sound
Adds kasra to indicate genitive case
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
This letter at the start turns what follows into a question, like adding a question mark in English

steps.m: And did...? (asking about something mentioned before)
steps.n: Adds و to connect this question to what was said before

steps.m: And did not...? (asking about something that didn't happen)
steps.n: Adds لَمْ to show we're asking about something that didn't happen in the past
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added ت at beginning to show 'you/she' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you are/she is (in a conditional statement)
steps.n: The word loses its middle و and final ن because it's in a conditional statement (after لَمْ in the ayah)
Connected the root letters together
The ي at the end shows it's talking to a female (you)
Added كُمْ at the end to show the action is directed towards multiple people
The و makes it mean 'someone who does this action a lot'
Changed from singular 'messenger' to plural by shortening middle vowel and changing end vowel pattern - a common way to make plurals in Arabic
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing something
Added كُم at the end to show the messengers belong to multiple people being spoken to
Added feminine ending to make it into a noun meaning 'a clear proof'
Added feminine plural ending to show multiple proofs
Added both the preposition بِ (with) and the definite article ال (the) at once
The kasra at the end shows this noun follows the preposition bi-
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes after fatha - this is a common Arabic spelling rule

steps.m: they said (the keepers of Hell responding to those in Hell)
steps.n: Added وا to show many people (they) did the action. In this ayah, this refers to the keepers of Hell responding to those who are asking for help.

This is a particle used for strong affirmation, especially in response to a negative statement. Here it's responding to the previous negative question 'Did not your messengers come to you?' confirming that yes, they did come.
Added an alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the end of the word. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas - this makes it easier to pronounce

steps.m: they spoke/said (the keepers of Hell responding to those in Hell)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action. This refers to the keepers of Hell responding to the people in Hell asking for relief.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed to command form with اُ prefix. The final و drops in command form.
Added وا to make the command plural - addressing multiple people
Added فَ at beginning to connect this command with what came before. The اُ changes to ٱ after فَ.

Added fatha to show correct pronunciation. This is a particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's negating دُعٰٓؤُا۟ (the calling/supplication) that comes after it.

Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects to the previous statement where the disbelievers were told to make supplication, and this phrase tells us that their supplication is useless.

steps.m: to call out to someone or make a request
act of calling out (owned by الكافرين)
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun showing the action itself. This word belongs to 'the disbelievers' (الكافرين) in this verse.

steps.m: act of calling out (owned by الكافرين)
steps.n: Added hamza at the end to make the word stable when adding endings
The word got a 'their' ending (وا) to show it belongs to multiple people

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changes from a verb (to reject) to a noun meaning 'rejecter'
Added ين to make it plural (more than one)
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific and َ at the end because it's being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: except (what comes after this word is excluded from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: This is a special word that shows an exception. It tells us that what comes after it is not included in what was just talked about. In this ayah, it's used to show that the disbelievers' call will only result in being lost.
Basic preposition showing location or containment
The ى extends the meaning to show being fully within something. In this ayah, it shows the disbelievers are completely in a state of misguidance.

steps.m: to wander off the right path

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from a past action (went astray) to a noun meaning the state of being lost
Added tanween kasra to make it indefinite (a/any state of being lost)

steps.m: in a state of being lost (in this ayah, describes the state of the disbelievers)
steps.n: The alif (ـٰ) was written differently but means the same thing - being lost/misguided. The -in ending shows it's describing a state.
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'surely' in English

steps.m: indeed we (subject of لَنَنصُرُ/we will surely help)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end shows this is about 'we/us'. This connects to the next word لَنَنصُرُ (we will surely help) where 'we' are the ones doing the helping
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding first person plural prefix
Adding 'u' sound at the end to show this is the main action

steps.m: surely we will help (promise to اللَّهِ's messengers)
steps.n: The لَ at the beginning shows this is a strong promise from Allah about helping His messengers

steps.m: to send or deliver a message

steps.n: The و after س changes it from 'sending' to 'one who sends/delivers'
The word changed from singular to plural by changing the middle vowel from 'u' to 'u' and removing the long vowel 'oo'
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb ننصر (we support/aid)
Added نا at the end to show these are our messengers. This word is being acted upon by the verb ننصر (we support/aid) in the ayah
Added ن at the end to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال at the beginning and doubled the ذ to make it mean 'those who' instead of just 'who'
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement about helping the messengers
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added alif after hamza to show strong or firm belief (Form IV verb pattern)

steps.m: they believed (connected to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word describes الَّذِينَ (those who) in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing location or time. In this ayah, it shows when Allah will support the believers.

steps.m: in/within/during (of ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - the worldly life)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (the worldly life) to show when Allah will give support.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed the word into a noun meaning 'life' by adding و and ة. The و replaces one of the ي letters to make it easier to pronounce.
Added ال to make it specific ('the life') and ِ to show it's connected to the word الدُّنْيَا (worldly) that comes after it, forming the phrase 'the worldly life'

steps.m: to be close or near to something
to be close or near to something
steps.n: Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added vowels and alif at end to make it a noun meaning 'the world'

steps.m: the worldly life (in ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - the worldly life)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and doubled the د letter because it comes after ال. In this ayah, it's describing 'life' (ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ), meaning specifically this worldly life as opposed to the afterlife.
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun form
Added fatha at the end to show this is the time when يَقُومُ (stands/rises) happens
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement about helping the believers
This is the basic concept of standing or rising up
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he stands/rises (in the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: The يَ at the start shows this is happening now or in the future. The middle letter changed from وَ to ُو because it's a special type of verb. The ُ at the end shows the action is happening.
Added vowels and ي to create the singular form meaning 'witness' before making it plural
Changes from one witness (شَهِيد) to many witnesses (أَشْهَاد) - like changing 'witness' to 'witnesses' in English
The ال makes it specific, and the ُ shows it's doing the action of standing/rising
Basic noun form of root meaning 'day', with vowel marks added
Adding fatha at the end shows this word is being used as a direct object
This is the basic negative particle that makes something not happen. In this ayah, it negates the verb يَنفَعُ (benefits), making it mean 'does not benefit'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he benefits/helps (subject of لَا - does not)
steps.n: The يَ at the start shows it's happening now or in the future. The ُ at the end shows this is the doer of the action. In this ayah, it comes after لَا which means 'does not', so together they mean 'does not benefit'.

steps.m: to do wrong, to be unjust

steps.n: Changes from a verb (action) to a noun describing someone who does that action
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
Doubling the ظ makes the wrongdoing more intense
Makes the word plural and shows it's being acted upon by the verb يَنفَعُ (benefits/profits) which is negated by لا

steps.m: to excuse or defend someone from blame

steps.n: Added مَ at beginning to make it a noun meaning 'the thing that excuses', added ِ in middle and ة at end to make it a specific instance
Added ُ to show this is the main subject being talked about in the sentence

steps.m: their excuse (referring to the wrongdoers mentioned as ٱلظَّالِمِينَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this excuse belongs to the wrongdoers mentioned earlier in the verse
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Added هُمْ to show who it's for - in this case 'them' refers back to الظَّالِمِينَ (the wrongdoers) mentioned earlier
Changed the ending to ُ to connect smoothly with the next word ٱللَّعْنَةُ (the curse)

steps.m: to curse or remove from mercy

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to a noun showing one instance of cursing
Makes the word specific/definite by adding 'the'
Added damma (u sound) at the end to show this word is doing the action
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to the wrongdoers (ٱلظَّالِمِينَ) mentioned earlier who are being cursed.
Changed from a verb meaning 'to be bad' into a noun meaning 'badness' by adding damma (u sound)
Added elongation mark (ٓ) over و to show long vowel sound

steps.m: evil (subject of this part of the ayah)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject

steps.m: to dwell, to move about in a place

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning the place where dwelling happens
Added the definite article to specify a particular home
Added emphasis by doubling the د
Added kasra to show this word is connected to something else in the sentence
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis, like saying 'most certainly' instead of just 'certainly'
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to make someone/something come or give
steps.n: Adding ءَا at the beginning makes the meaning about causing someone else to do the action, like changing 'come' to 'make come' or 'give'

steps.m: to make someone/something come or give
steps.n: The ى changes to يْ before adding نا
Adding نَا shows that 'we' did the action. In this ayah, we gave something to Moses.

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who received the Torah)
steps.n: This is a proper name that came into Arabic from ancient Egyptian (through Hebrew). The name may have originally meant 'son' or 'born of' in ancient Egyptian. In the Quran, this refers to the prophet who received the Torah and led the Children of Israel.

steps.m: to guide or show the right way

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to guide' into the noun 'guidance'
Adding ال makes the word definite, like adding 'the' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adding hamza alif prefix makes it mean 'to cause someone to inherit'. The word بَنِىٓ (Children) is who received the inheritance.
The word changed from 'he made inherit' to 'we made inherit' by adding نا at the end, showing that 'we' did the action instead of 'he'

steps.m: and we made (someone) inherit (connected to ءَاتَيْنَا - we gave)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect with previous statement about giving Moses guidance

steps.m: to build or establish a foundation

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to create basic past tense verb form
The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top when it comes before another word it's connected to. This shows it's linked to the word إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ (Israel) that comes after it.
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew meaning 'one who struggles with God'. It refers to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب) who was given this name after wrestling with an angel.
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object (receiver) of the verb وَأَوْرَثْنَا (and We made inherit). The full phrase بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ means 'Children of Israel'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (wrote) to a noun (book/writing) by adding alif
Adds 'the' (ال) to make it a specific book, and the ending shows something is happening to it
Changes from verb 'to guide' into the noun 'guidance'
Adds ً to show this is any guidance (not specific)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels and added ى to make a noun meaning 'reminder' that pairs with the word هُدًى (guidance) before it

steps.m: and a reminder (paired with هُدًى/guidance for لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبٰبِ/those of understanding)
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect it with هُدًى (guidance) as two things given to those of understanding

steps.m: possessors of, people of, those who have
steps.n: This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It's always used to show possession or belonging

steps.m: for those who have (connected to ذِكْرَىٰ - a reminder)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this word is connected to ذِكْرَىٰ (a reminder) - meaning this reminder is specifically for these people
Adding damma makes it into a noun meaning the core/essence
Plural form made by adding أَ at start and changing middle to اب (أَلْبَاب is a broken plural of لُبّ)
Added ٱلْ to make it specific

steps.m: of the minds/intellects (possessed by أُو۟لِى - people of)
steps.n: Added ِ to show this word belongs to أُو۟لِى (people of) in the phrase أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبٰبِ (people of understanding)
Adding vowels to make it a command form

steps.m: so be patient! (connected to وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ - Allah's promise is true)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this command is a result of what was mentioned before - that Allah's promise is true, so therefore be patient
Added kasra under the alif to help with pronunciation
Added shadda to strengthen the emphasis. This particle makes the following phrase وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ (Allah's promise is truth) more emphatic.

steps.m: to promise or make a commitment

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun by adding sukun on middle letter and removing final vowel
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed) in the phrase 'indeed, the promise of Allah'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped in this special word.
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of وَعْدَ (promise). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since this is how Allah's name is always written.
Root letters ح-ق-ق were joined together with vowels and the middle ق was doubled (with shadda) to make the noun 'truth'

steps.m: a truth/reality (subject of إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this is the subject of the sentence 'Indeed, Allah's promise is truth'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show seeking or requesting the action
Changed from 'he sought' to 'you seek!' by changing the vowels to make it a command to one person

steps.m: and seek forgiveness! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و to connect with previous command (فَٱصْبِرْ - be patient)

steps.m: to commit a fault or sin

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to the root letters to make it mean 'a sin' instead of 'to sin'
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by something
Added كَ to show the sin belongs to the person being addressed
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is what forgiveness is being sought for, connecting it to the verb اسْتَغْفِرْ (seek forgiveness)
Added كَ to show this sin belongs to 'you' (masculine singular)
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to add emphasis, while the vowel changes and sukun make it a command
Adding و connects this command with the previous commands in the ayah (فَٱصْبِرْ and وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ)
Added vowel marks to turn the root into a noun meaning the act of praising

steps.m: with/by the praise (of رَبِّكَ your Lord)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with/by' and kasra at end because it's connected to the word رَبِّكَ (your Lord) that comes after it
The word starts with 'bi' meaning 'with' - it connects to 'praise' to show doing something with praise
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone's main role or characteristic - they are the complete caretaker
The kasra ending shows this lord belongs to or is connected to something
Adding كَ shows the lord belongs to 'you'. This word follows بِحَمْدِ (with praise of) in the ayah.

steps.m: to become dark/to come in the evening

steps.n: Adds vowels and shadda to make a noun meaning evening/nighttime
Added ال to make it specific, like saying 'the evening' instead of just 'evening'

steps.m: in the evening (paired with وَٱلْإِبْكٰرِ - and the early morning)
steps.n: Added بِ to show this happens during/in the evening. This word is paired with وَٱلْإِبْكٰرِ to show doing something both in the evening and early morning.

steps.m: to be early, to do something in the morning
he was early/did something in morning
steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed to a noun form that refers to the time of morning
Added 'and' plus 'the' to specify this particular morning time

steps.m: and the early morning (in relation to العَشِىِّ)
steps.n: The word gets a special ending (-i) because it's paired with 'evening' to mean 'morning and evening'
Adding the shadda (doubled ن) changes this from a conditional word into an emphasis word. This word makes the statement that follows it stronger and more certain.
This is the base form of the relative pronoun that points to someone or something being described
Added ال with shadda to make it definite and specific
Added ين to make it plural. This word refers to the people who argue (يُجٰدِلُونَ) in the verse.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he argued back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Added alif to show the action goes back and forth between people

steps.m: he argues back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra

steps.m: they argue back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action, and changed alif to alif with bar (ٰ) for proper writing
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects to the word ءَايٰتِ (signs/verses) to show what is being argued about.
The alif at the end is lengthened when stopping at this word, but doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to show, make clear, be apparent

steps.n: Adding ة changes this into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Added ات to make it plural. This is a special plural form for feminine nouns.
Changed alif to alif maksura and added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by Allah (اللَّهِ) in the phrase 'signs of Allah'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Adding 'al' makes it refer to a specific deity rather than any deity
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is being described by another word
Basic noun form meaning 'other than' or 'without'
Adds the preposition بِ which shows how something is done or the means by which it happens
Adds the genitive case ending ِ because it follows the preposition بِ
Changed the word into a noun showing strong authority or clear proof of something
The ٍ shows this is any authority in general, not a specific one. In this ayah, it refers to the lack of authority that people who argue about Allah's signs have.
Changed from basic root 'to come' into past tense 'he came' by adding fatha marks
Added alif maksura mark (ٰ) to show the long vowel sound clearly
Added هُمْ at the end to show who received the action (them)

steps.m: there is not (in صُدُورِهِمْ - their chests)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it. In this ayah, it's negating what is in their chests, connecting to the word صُدُورِهِمْ (their chests).
Basic preposition showing something is inside or within something else
The ى at the end makes it point to something specific. Here it points to صُدُورِهِمْ (their hearts/chests) in the ayah
The root letters get vowels added to make a noun meaning 'chest' - the place where feelings come from
Changes the word to mean many chests/breasts by changing the vowels and adding و

steps.m: their chests/breasts (that contain what they feel)
steps.n: Adds هِمْ at the end to show these chests belong to them. In this ayah, it refers to what's in their hearts/chests - their feelings of pride.

steps.m: except/only (excluding كِبْرٌ from what's in their hearts)
steps.n: This is a particle that excludes something from what came before it. In this ayah, it excludes 'arrogance' (كِبْرٌ) from what's in their hearts. It's like saying 'nothing is in their hearts EXCEPT arrogance.'

steps.m: to be big, great, or prideful

steps.n: The kasra and sukun pattern changes this from an action into the feeling or state itself. In this ayah, it refers to what is in their hearts (صُدُورِهِمْ).

steps.m: pride (subject of what is in their hearts)
steps.n: The tanwin dammah (ٌ) shows this is the subject of what exists in their hearts, making it indefinite (any pride, not specific pride)
The base word مَا is strengthened with a shadda (ّ) to emphasize its meaning. In this ayah, it's used with إِلَّا to mean 'nothing but' or 'only', negating كِبْرٌ (pride/arrogance).
This is a pronoun that refers back to 'those who dispute' (الَّذِينَ يُجٰدِلُونَ) mentioned earlier in the verse. It shows who is being talked about.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Added preposition بِ to show means or instrument
Added kasra at end to show genitive case after preposition بِ
Added هِ to show possession - 'its reaching'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added است at the start to show actively seeking something for yourself - like asking for protection
Changed from 'he sought protection' to 'you seek protection!' by making it a command - dropping the middle و and changing vowels
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then', connecting it to the previous statement
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one true deity, making it a proper noun for God
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
Added بِ at beginning to show doing something with or by means of Allah. Also added hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) which helps pronunciation when starting with ال
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to 'indeed'. This emphasizes what comes after it.

steps.m: indeed he/it (connected to هُوَ which comes after)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end to show who/what is being referred to. This هُۥ refers to the word هُوَ that comes right after in the ayah.

steps.m: He (referring to Allah as the All-Hearer in this ayah)
steps.n: This is the pronoun for 'he' that refers back to Allah in this ayah. It connects the description that follows (All-Hearer, All-Seer) to Allah.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to experience hearing'
Changed from past tense verb 'heard' to a noun meaning 'intense hearer' by adding ي and changing vowels
Added ال (The) to make it a title for Allah, with shadda showing He hears everything
Added damma to show this is a subject/attribute
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
The pattern فَعِيل is often used for intensive adjectives, showing someone who does something extensively or has a strong quality
The word gained 'Al' (ال) at the start to make it 'The All-Seeing' - one of Allah's special names that shows He sees everything perfectly
Added U-sound (damma) at the end because this word describes who هُوَ (He) is in the sentence

steps.m: to create or make something from nothing

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'create' to 'creation' by adding vowels. The damma (ُ) shows this is the subject - the thing being described as greater
Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that this is definitely true - the creation of the heavens and earth is certainly greater
Adding fathas creates the basic past tense verb pattern
The root word changed from a verb (to rise up) into a plural noun meaning multiple heavens by adding ات at the end and changing the middle letter to ا
Adding the definite article to make it 'the heavens'

steps.m: the heavens (possessed by/connected to something after it)
steps.n: The word gets a special ending (-i) to show it belongs to or connects with what comes after it
Added و at beginning to connect this with السماوات (the heavens) mentioned before it

steps.m: and the earth (object of comparison with السماوات)
steps.n: Gets an 'i' ending because it's connected to 'and the heavens' with 'and'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Makes it comparative form meaning 'greater than'. In this ayah, it's comparing the creation of heavens and earth to the creation of mankind.
This is a preposition used here to compare two things: the creation of heavens and earth (خَلْقُ) with the creation of mankind (خَلْقِ). It shows that the first is greater than the second.

steps.m: to create or form something from nothing
creation (the act or result of creating)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to turn the action into a noun meaning either the act of creating or what is created
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is connected to 'mankind' (الناس) that comes after it, like saying 'creation of mankind'

steps.m: to be social, to keep company with others

steps.n: Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from a verb meaning 'to be social' to a noun meaning 'people' by dropping the first letter and adding a long 'aa' sound after the first root letter
Added ال to make it definite (like 'the' in English), doubled the ن with shadda, and added kasra at the end because this word follows مِنْ in the ayah

steps.m: but (emphasizing the contrast with what came before)
steps.n: This is an emphatic particle that shows a strong contrast. The ّ (shadda) at the end makes it stronger.

steps.m: and yet/however (connecting to خَلْقُ while contrasting with أَكْثَرَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this contrast with the previous statement. This shows that even though the first part is true, the second part is also true.
Added vowels to make it mean 'it became many' in the past
Added أَ at the beginning to make it mean 'more/greater', comparing two things (like saying 'bigger than' in English)

steps.m: to socialize or be familiar with others

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'people' by adding a long alif and changing the vowels
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it follows the word مِنْ (from/than). The ن gets doubled (shadda) which is common in this word.
This is a negative particle that makes the following verb يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) into its opposite meaning (they do not know)
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at the start and changed vowels to show it's happening in the present/future
Added ون to show this is about multiple people. This word is negated by لَا (not) in the ayah.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation. This particle can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's used with يَسْتَوِى (are equal) to negate it.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. The word negates يَسْتَوِى (are equal) in this ayah.
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show doing something to or for oneself

steps.m: he becomes equal (comparing ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ - the blind and the seeing)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and removing initial ا. This word is comparing two things in the ayah: the blind and the seeing.
Changes the final letter to ى which is common for this type of verb
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this is something that happens to someone
Changed the word to describe the quality of being blind rather than the action of becoming blind
The long alif (ى) with a small alif (ٰ) on top shows this is a permanent state of being blind, not just temporary blindness

steps.m: the blind one (contrasted with ٱلْبَصِيرُ/the seeing one)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to a specific blind person or type of person. In this ayah, it's contrasted with ٱلْبَصِيرُ (the seeing one).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who sees clearly/is insightful (opposite of الْأَعْمَىٰ)
steps.n: Forms intensive adjective showing someone with strong ability to see/understand. Contrasts with الْأَعْمَىٰ (the blind) in this verse.

steps.m: the one who sees clearly/is insightful
steps.n: Adding definite article to make the word definite

steps.m: and the one who sees clearly/is insightful
steps.n: Adding conjunction to connect with previous phrase
Starting with the base demonstrative word that points to something
Adding ال and ي changes it from pointing at something to describing who or what does something
Adding ن makes it refer to many people or things instead of just one
Adding و at the start connects it to what came before. The word is followed by ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) which tells us what these people did.

steps.m: to cause to feel secure/to believe
steps.n: Adding alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
This word is part of what's being compared in يَسْتَوِى (are equal). The وا shows it's talking about a group of people who believed.
Added a-a-a vowels to make it 'he did' in the past tense
Changing the middle vowel from fatha to kasra makes it mean 'to work/act' rather than just 'to do'
Adds 'and' (wa) at start + changes to 'they' (plural) form

steps.m: and they did/performed (connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ - believed)
steps.n: Added plural marker وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) describing the same group of people.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Added alif after first root letter and kasra after second letter to show who/what has this quality
Added ات at the end to make it feminine plural
Added ال at beginning to make it definite and doubled the ص with shadda

steps.m: the good/righteous ones (feminine plural) (object of عَمِلُوا)
steps.n: Added kasra at end to show this is the object of the verb عَمِلُوا (they did/performed)
This is a basic negation particle that means 'not' or 'no'. It's used to negate what comes after it.

steps.m: and not (connects to previous statement about those who believe)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negation to the previous statement about believers and good deeds, showing contrast with what comes next.

steps.m: to harm or make something bad

steps.n: Added vowels (a) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he harmed'
Added مُ at start to show 'the one who', middle letter و changed to ي, and vowels changed to make 'doer' pattern
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the one who does evil (subject of يَسْتَوِى)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is the subject of يَسْتَوِى (is equal to). Changed spelling of ي with lengthening mark.
The ي makes it into an adjective describing something that has the quality of being small in amount

steps.m: little (describes how much تَتَذَكَّرُونَ you remember)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this word describes how much of the action happens. It's connected to تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (you remember) in the next part of the verse.
This is a relative pronoun that connects ideas
The shadda shows this word connects to تَتَذَكَّرُونَ (remember), telling us what is being remembered
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Doubling the middle letter changes 'remember' to 'remind' - making someone else remember

Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject
This is the base word - Allah's proper name. It's a unique word that doesn't come from a three-letter root.
Added ل at the beginning which means 'for' or 'to'. The first ا of الله is dropped when we add ل, which is a special rule for this word.

Added the vowel marks: ِ under the first ل (kasra), ّ on the second ل (shadda), and ِ under the ه (kasra). The shadda shows that the second ل is doubled. In this ayah, this word shows that all praise belongs to Allah.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, raise up
steps.n: The basic concept of nurturing or sustaining

steps.m: Lord (one who cares for and sustains everything)
steps.n: Added shadda to show this is someone who does this action perfectly. Like how a teacher teaches, a Lord perfectly cares for everything.
Added kasra at the end to show this word owns or possesses what comes after it (العالمين - the worlds)
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes from a verb (knew) to a noun meaning 'world' by adding an alif after the first letter
Made plural to show multiple worlds. This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord), forming the phrase 'Lord of the worlds'.
Made definite with ٱلْ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense form

steps.m: say! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the disbelievers)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by: 1) dropping the middle weak letter و, 2) adding damma on first letter, 3) adding sukun on last letter. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the disbelievers, as shown by the next part of the ayah.
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to statements, like adding 'indeed' or 'verily' in English

steps.m: indeed I (subject of نُهِيتُ - I was forbidden)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show this refers to 'I'. The ي combines with the نّ to become نِّى. This word connects to نُهِيتُ (I was forbidden) which comes right after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to prevent or stop someone from doing something

steps.n: Changed to passive voice to show the action was done to someone

steps.m: I was prevented/forbidden (subject of أَنْ أَعْبُدَ - that I worship)
steps.n: Added تُ at the end to show 'I' was the one who was forbidden. This connects to the next phrase 'that I worship'.
This is a connecting word that joins نُهِيتُ (I was forbidden) to what was forbidden (أَعْبُدَ - to worship). It's like saying 'I was forbidden that I worship...'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed from past tense (a-a-a) to present tense (u-u) pattern, with first letter getting sukoon

steps.m: I worship (after أَنْ in the ayah)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' and changed final vowel to fatha because it comes after أَنْ in the ayah
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it point to something specific that was mentioned before

steps.m: those who/which (referring to the false gods that are worshipped)
steps.n: Added نَ at the end to show we're talking about many things or people, not just one. In this ayah, it refers to the false gods that people worship besides Allah.
Changed vowels to present tense pattern. The sukun on first letter prepares it for prefixes.
Prefix تَ changes verb to 2nd person 'you'

steps.m: you (all) call/invite (those you worship besides Allah)
steps.n: Suffix نَ makes it plural. This refers to the false deities being called upon besides Allah, as mentioned in the surrounding ayah.
This is a preposition that shows separation or starting point. In this ayah, it connects the word دُونِ (besides/other than) with اللَّهِ (Allah), helping to express 'besides/other than Allah'.
The kasra (ِ) under the م shows this word is connecting to what comes after it
Adding damma (ُ) to make it a complete word
Added kasra at the end to connect it with the following word اللَّهِ (Allah)
Changed first letter and added ل with shadda to make it specifically refer to Allah, the One True God
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of دُونِ (besides/other than) in the phrase مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ (besides Allah)
Starting with the basic particle لم which marks time or negation, and adding fatha to connect the letters
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more specific

steps.m: when (followed by جَآءَنِىَ - came to me)
steps.n: Adding alif at the end makes this word specifically mean 'when' in time expressions. Here it introduces what happened when something came.
The root letters changed to make 'he came' - the middle letter ي became ا
The long alif adds emphasis to show he definitely/surely came
Added نِى at the end to show the meaning 'to me'

steps.m: to make clear or to separate things

steps.n: Changed from a verb meaning 'to make clear' into an adjective meaning 'clear' - like changing 'clarify' to 'clear'
Makes the word plural using the feminine plural ending ات. The alif with line above (ٰ) is just a different way of writing a long a sound.
Makes the noun definite by adding the definite article. This word is the subject of جَآءَ (came) in this ayah.
Adds a 'u' sound at the end to show this word is doing the action (the proofs are the ones that came)
This is a basic preposition showing separation or distance. In this ayah, it works with دُونِ to mean 'besides' or 'other than' when talking about not worshipping false gods.
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
The doubled letter shows this is someone who does a lot of nurturing/caring
Adding kasra at the end makes this a call/cry to one's Lord
Adding ي at the end shows possession - 'my Lord'
Adding fatha at the end helps with pronunciation when connecting to next word
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' was the one who was commanded

steps.m: And I was commanded (connected to نُهِيتُ - I was forbidden)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this with the previous statement about being forbidden
This particle connects to the verb أَعْبُدَ (worship) to show what was forbidden. In this ayah, it shows that the speaker was forbidden from worshipping those they call upon besides Allah.
This root carries the basic meaning of peaceful submission or surrender
Adding the short vowels 'fatha' (َ) turns the root letters into a past tense verb meaning 'he did something'
Adding أَ at the start makes the action more direct and complete - showing active, willing submission
Changed to passive voice - shows someone/something else caused the submission
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create the intensive noun meaning 'lord'
Added kasra to show this word is connected to something after it
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is for/to the lord. This word is connected to الْعٰلَمِينَ (the worlds) which comes after it.
Forms a noun meaning 'world' or 'creation' from the root. This pattern is used for comprehensive or all-encompassing concepts.
Adds the sound masculine plural ending to show multiple worlds or creations

steps.m: the worlds, all creation (owned by رَبّ)
steps.n: Adds the definite article to specify these particular worlds. This word is possessed by رَبّ (Lord) in this ayah.
Adds fatha at the end to show this word is in the accusative case
This is a pronoun that points to someone being talked about. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who is described in the words that follow as the one who created humans from dust.

steps.m: The one who/which - connects descriptions to what's being described
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that introduces a description or explanation

steps.m: to create, form, or shape something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word describes what 'He (Allah) who...' did.
The كُم at the end means 'you all' as the object of the verb

steps.m: from (showing what خَلَقَكُم/He created you is made from)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing source or origin. In this ayah, it connects to خَلَقَكُم (He created you) to show what humans were created from.

steps.m: from (showing what خَلَقَكُم/He created you is made from)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a word ending in ن or tanween. This is just a pronunciation helper and doesn't change the meaning.
Shows what something is made of or its basic substance
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) because it comes after the word مِن (from/of). This shows it's any dust/soil, not a specific one.

steps.m: then (connects the stages of human creation in sequence)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another. In this ayah, it connects different stages of how humans are created - from dust, then from a drop, then from a clinging substance, and so on.
This is a basic preposition that connects two ideas by showing where something comes from. In this ayah, it shows that Allah created humans from dust.

steps.m: to drip or flow in drops
steps.n: The root letters form the basic concept of dripping or flowing
Adding ة turns the concept of dripping into a single drop
Added shadda on ن because it comes after مِن in the ayah

steps.m: from a drop of fluid (after مِن)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra because it comes after the word مِن (from) in this ayah
Basic form of the word that shows something comes after something else

steps.m: then, thereafter (connects ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ with مِن نُّطْفَةٍ)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) and shadda (ّ) to show this is a complete connecting word. In this ayah, it connects the stages of human creation, showing how Allah creates us first from dust, then from a drop of fluid.

steps.m: from (showing that عَلَقَةٍ 'a clinging substance' comes after this stage)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows the starting point or source. Here it's used to show the sequence of human development stages, connecting نُّطْفَةٍ (a drop) to عَلَقَةٍ (a clinging substance).
Changed from basic root meaning 'to cling' into a noun meaning 'a clinging thing' by adding fatha marks
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of something that clings (in this context, referring to a blood clot)
Added tanwin kasra because this word comes after the preposition مِنْ (from) in the ayah
This is a connecting word (particle) that shows one thing happens after another, with some time passing in between

steps.m: then/next (showing sequence with مِن نُّطْفَةٍ coming after تُرَابٍ)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce it correctly: ُ (u) on ث and َ (a) with ّ (double) on م
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The form IV pattern makes it causative - instead of just 'exit', it means 'make exit'. This is a verb, not a noun, so it doesn't have case endings.
Adding the attached pronoun كم which serves as the direct object, meaning 'you all' (masculine plural)
Changed from describing an action (being small and gentle) to naming something that is small and gentle (a child)

steps.m: a child (being brought forth by يُخْرِجُكُمْ)
steps.n: The ًا shows this word is receiving the action of يُخْرِجُكُمْ (He brings you forth) - like saying 'He brings forth a child'
This is a connecting word (particle) that shows one thing happens after another. It's not made from a root.

steps.m: then (connects events in this ayah about stages of human creation)
steps.n: Added the vowel marks ُ (damma) and ّ (shadda) to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'thumma'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changes to present tense 'you reach' by adding تَ at start and changing vowels
Makes the verb plural to show many people are doing it

steps.m: so that you all may reach (أَشُدَّكُمْ - your maturity)
steps.n: Adds لِ at the beginning to show purpose - this explains why something happens
Changed from basic verb 'to be strong' into passive verb 'was made strong/firm'

steps.m: full strength/maturity (in لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ - so that you reach)
steps.n: Added أَ to show this is the peak or fullness of strength/maturity. This word is what is being reached in the phrase 'so that you reach'
Added fatha because this word is the object (thing being reached) in the phrase 'so that you reach'
Adds كم to indicate possession by 2nd person plural masculine

steps.m: then (connects this stage of creation to the previous stage)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another. In this ayah, it connects different stages of human creation, showing how Allah creates humans step by step.
Connected the root letters with damma to form the basic verb stem
The تَ prefix shows that 'you' are doing the action
The وا ending changes it from singular 'you' to plural 'you all'

steps.m: so that you all may become (purpose of خَلَقَكُم - He created you)
steps.n: The لِ shows this is the purpose or goal of Allah creating humans, mentioned earlier in the verse with خَلَقَكُم (He created you)
Root letters ش-ي-خ (aging) become شَيْخ (elderly person) by adding fatha and sukun marks. Like changing 'old' into 'an old person'
Changed the vowels to ُ (u sound) and added و to make it refer to many elderly people
Added ًا at the end to show this describes what they will become in the phrase 'so that you become elderly people'
This is a preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or belonging to something
The كُم suffix shows this applies to a group of people being talked to

steps.m: and from you all (connected to مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ - who dies)
steps.n: The وَ connects this phrase to the next part of the ayah about 'who dies', showing these are related ideas

steps.m: whoever (subject of يُتَوَفَّىٰ - is caused to die)
steps.n: Added emphasis by doubling the م letter with a shadda. This word connects to يُتَوَفَّىٰ (is caused to die) in this ayah, showing who the action happens to.
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning more specific to taking life/causing death
Adding تَ at the start shows someone is doing this action to another - like taking their soul
Adding يُ at the beginning shows this is happening to someone rather than someone doing it

steps.m: from (shows تُرَابٍ 'dust' is the starting point)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it shows that Allah created humans starting from dust.
This form makes it mean a time or place before something
Adding these vowel marks makes it a noun meaning 'before'
Adding the damma (U sound) at the end shows this word is in the nominative case

steps.m: to reach, arrive at, or attain something
steps.n: This is the basic past tense form
Changes from root letters to past tense 'he' form
Changes from past to present tense by adding ت at start
Adds لِ to show purpose - means 'so that' or 'in order to'
Adds وا to make the verb plural - means multiple people are doing the action
Adds 'and' to connect this with what came before
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and make it a noun meaning the specific time itself rather than the action of setting time

steps.m: an appointed time (object of لِتَبْلُغُوا/that you may reach)
steps.n: The ًا at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb لِتَبْلُغُوا (that you may reach). It's like saying 'reach an appointed time' where 'appointed time' is what's being reached.
Added مُ at the beginning and changed the ending to show this is something that was named (like a person's name or title)
Added emphasis mark (shadda) on م and tanwin ending to show this is a specific appointed time or deadline
Starting with the basic word لعلّ which shows hope or expectation
Adding و at the beginning connects this with the previous phrase

steps.m: and so that you may (connected to تَعْقِلُونَ - you use reason)
steps.n: Adding كُمْ at the end shows this applies to all of you. This connects to the next word تَعْقِلُونَ (you use reason) to complete the meaning: 'and so that you may use reason'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense and adds 'you' as the doer
Changes from singular 'you' to plural 'you all'

steps.m: you all use reason (addressing the audience of the Quran)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is addressing multiple people. In this ayah, it's addressing all people who hear/read the Quran's message about Allah's signs of creation.

steps.m: He (refers to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a pronoun that refers to Allah. It's used at the start of the ayah to emphasize that it is Allah who gives life and causes death, as mentioned in the words that follow (ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ).
This is the base form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ٱلَّ at the beginning to make it definite and emphasize that it's pointing to something specific. In this ayah, it's describing هُوَ (He), referring to Allah.

steps.m: he gives life (connected to وَيُمِيتُ - and causes death)
steps.n: The root ح-ي-ي (life) becomes يُحْيِي (he gives life) by adding يُ at the start to mean 'he' in present tense
Changed spelling to use ى and ۦ instead of ي letters, but pronunciation stays the same
Added vowel marks to connect the letters into a basic noun form
Changed from 'death' to 'one who causes death' by changing the middle vowel from 'aw' to 'ee'
Added يُ at start to show 'he' is doing it, and ُ at end to show it's happening now

steps.m: and he causes to die (connected to يُحْىِۦ - he gives life)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this word with the previous word يُحْىِۦ (he gives life), showing Allah's power over both life and death
This is a time word that introduces a condition - it shows something will happen after something else happens
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this to what came before and show one thing follows another. In this ayah, it connects to قَضَىٰٓ (He decrees) to show what happens after the decree.
The root letters got vowels (fathas) and the last letter changed to ى to show it's in the past tense, meaning 'he decided'
Added an alif symbol (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced like a long a sound
Added a small alif (ٓ) above to show the sound is stretched slightly when reading

steps.m: a matter/affair (object of قَضَىٰٓ - he decreed)
steps.n: Changed from basic root letters to a noun by adding vowels - now means 'a matter/affair' instead of the action of commanding
The ًا (tanween) at the end shows this word is receiving the action of 'decreeing' from قَضَىٰٓ (he decreed)
This is a basic emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before

steps.m: then only/just (connects to يَقُولُ - he says)
steps.n: Added ما at the end which combines with إِنَّ to mean 'only/just'. This word connects to يَقُولُ (he says) in the ayah, showing what happens next.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowels. The و stays because it's part of the root.
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced

steps.m: to/for him (connected to يَقُولُ - he says)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ (him) to show who the action is for. This word connects to يَقُولُ (he says) in the ayah, showing what he says is 'for him/to him'.
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command - damma on first letter, sukun on middle letter
Final form of the command 'be!' - weak letter و is dropped because of command form
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ

steps.m: and so it becomes (result of كُن - 'Be!')
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result of Allah's command كُن (Be!)
Added أَ at the beginning to turn this into a question

steps.m: have you not? (addressing the Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added مْ at the end to make the following verb negative in the past tense. This combination أَلَمْ is commonly used to ask 'Haven't you?' or 'Don't you see?'
Basic past tense pattern (fa'ala) with fatha vowels to make 'he saw'
Changes from 'he saw' to 'you see' by adding تَ prefix

steps.m: you see/consider (in أَلَمْ تَرَ - have you not seen/considered)
steps.n: The ى at the end is dropped when it comes after لَمْ in the common phrase أَلَمْ تَرَ (have you not seen?)
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows direction or destination

steps.m: to, towards (connecting to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added ى at the end which is part of the standard spelling of this preposition. In this ayah, it connects to 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) who are arguing about Allah's signs.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that refers to someone or something
Added ال with a shadda to make it definite and emphasize its role as a connector
Added ين at the end to make it plural. This word refers to the people who are arguing about Allah's signs (as shown by يُجٰدِلُونَ - they argue/dispute)

steps.m: to argue or debate back and forth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adding ا shows two people are arguing with each other
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. The alif is written as ٰ when it comes after ج. They are arguing about Allah's signs/verses.
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it shows the topic/location of the arguing - within/concerning Allah's signs.
The ى and ٓ are spelling variations that don't change the meaning - they just show how to pronounce the word correctly with a long 'ee' sound

steps.m: a sign (something that shows or proves)
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning one single sign or proof
Changed to plural form to show multiple signs. This word connects to 'Allah' (اللَّهِ) in the ayah, showing these are Allah's signs specifically.
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to what comes after it (اللَّهِ)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ِ at the end shows it's being acted upon by the word before it.
This is a basic particle that shows emphasis or certainty
Adding ى with alif at the end changes this from an emphasis particle to a question word asking about manner. In this ayah, it's asking about how people are being turned away from the truth.

steps.m: to turn away or divert from a path

steps.n: Added vowels to make it mean 'he turned away' in the past tense
Changed vowels to make it passive, showing the action is being done to someone
Changed from past tense to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowels
Added ونَ to show multiple people are being turned away
Added ن to make it plural, like changing 'who' to 'who (multiple people)'
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific. The ل combines with ذ to make ذّ. This word is the subject (doer) of كَذَّبُوا (they denied) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows that the denial is stronger or done repeatedly

steps.m: they strongly denied/rejected (the Book and what We sent Our messengers with)
steps.n: Added the plural ending that shows a group of people did this action. This word connects to what follows it in the ayah (بِٱلْكِتٰبِ وَبِمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا بِهِۦ رُسُلَنَا) which tells us what they denied.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb

This pattern is often used for things that result from an action - a book is the result of writing. The word is in the genitive case because it comes after the preposition بِ (bi) in بِٱلْكِتٰبِ, where it is the object of 'they denied/belied'.
The definite article ال makes the noun definite ('the book' instead of 'a book')
The preposition بِ adds the meaning 'with' or 'by'

steps.m: with/by the book (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: The word means 'with/by the book' because of بِ at the start. It's what they denied (كَذَّبُوا) in this verse.
Added fatha to show basic pronunciation
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is done 'with' something
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase
Changed the final alif to an elongated alif (آ) to show emphasis in pronunciation
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Adding أَ (alif) at the start changes the meaning from 'send' to 'cause to send' - like the difference between 'send' and 'make someone send'
The نا at the end shows that 'we' did the action in the past
This is a basic preposition that shows how something is done or connected
Added the pronoun هِ to show what/who is being referred to
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changed the word to mean someone who carries messages as their role, like how 'send' becomes 'messenger'
Changed the word to show many messengers instead of just one by changing the vowels

steps.m: our messengers (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show these are 'our' messengers. This word is being denied/rejected by the disbelievers in this verse.
Added vowel marks to show this is a future particle that comes before verbs to show they will happen later. Here it comes before يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) to mean 'they will know'.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of what came before. It connects to the previous statement about those who denied the Book and messengers.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Adding يَ at the start changes it from past tense (he knew) to present tense (he knows)

steps.m: they know (referring to those who denied)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this refers to multiple people. In this ayah, it refers back to those who denied (الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا) the Book.
This is the basic form of the word that shows a point in time
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it in the ayah - the chains that will be around their necks
Changes from singular to broken plural form, meaning multiple chains
Adding ال makes it refer to specific chains (in this case, the ones mentioned in the ayah)

steps.m: the chains, the shackles (subject of يُسْحَبُونَ)
steps.n: The ُ shows this is the subject of the verb يُسْحَبُونَ (they are dragged) in this ayah
This is a basic preposition showing location - in this case showing the shackles are in/around their necks
Added a small alif (ٓ) at the end to show where to pause when reading. This doesn't change the meaning.
Adding the vowel marks ُ (damma) to show this is a basic noun meaning 'neck'
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple necks
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something

steps.m: their necks (referring to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the surah)
steps.n: Added هِمْ to show the necks belong to them (masculine plural)

steps.m: to link things together in a series

steps.n: Takes the root letters for 'linking' and repeats them to make the word for one chain
Changed the word pattern to show many chains instead of just one
Added ال to point to specific chains mentioned in the ayah
Added و to connect this with the previous word (ٱلْأَغْلٰلُ - the shackles)
Added ُ to show this is the subject of يُسْحَبُونَ (they are dragged). Also changed اَ to ٰ which is just a writing style difference.

steps.m: to drag or pull something along the ground

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to passive voice present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are being dragged

steps.m: in (something is inside ٱلْحَمِيمِ 'the scalding water')
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something. In this ayah, it shows the disbelievers are in scalding water.

steps.m: in (something is inside ٱلْحَمِيمِ 'the scalding water')
steps.n: The ي at the end changes to ى when written at the end of a word. This is just a writing change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show intense quality

steps.m: the boiling water (that will be used to punish the disbelievers)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end because it comes after في (in) in the ayah

steps.m: then (connecting فِى ٱلْحَمِيمِ with فِى ٱلنَّارِ)
steps.n: This word connects two events in sequence - first being thrown in boiling water, then in fire. The ُ (damma) and ّ (shadda) with َ (fatha) make the word stronger to emphasize this sequence.
This is a basic preposition showing location inside both 'the boiling water' and 'the fire' in this ayah
Added the kasra vowel mark (ِ) under ف to show how it's pronounced, and wrote the ي as ى since it's at the end of the word
Root letters changed to make the noun 'fire' - middle letter became long ا with fatha on first letter
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word في (in/within) in this ayah

steps.m: to burn or heat something intensely

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to passive voice by adding يُ at start and changing vowels to show this is happening to someone/something

steps.m: they are being burned/heated (in النَّارِ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added ون to show this is happening to multiple people. This refers to the disbelievers being burned in the Fire mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: then (connecting what follows to what came before)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows sequence of events, with some delay or gap between the actions. In this ayah, it connects to what happened before with what is about to be said to them.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive voice by adding kasra at start and changing middle و to ي
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose. Here it connects to the verb قِيلَ (it was said) to show who was spoken to.

steps.m: to/for them (object of قِيلَ - it was said)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show this refers to a group (them). This group is being addressed in the ayah when something is said to them.
Basic question word used to ask about location. In this ayah, it's used to ask the disbelievers about the location of their false deities.

steps.m: where (asking about false deities in تُشْرِكُونَ)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha on أ, sukun on ي, and fatha on ن. This word is followed by مَا كُنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ (what you used to associate [with Allah]) to form the complete question.
This is a connecting word that links أَيْنَ (where) with كُنتُمْ (you were). In questions, it helps make the question more specific.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he was' to 'you were' by adding تَ at the end. The middle و gets dropped to make it easier to say.

steps.m: you (all) were (asking about what you used to worship)
steps.n: Changed from singular 'you' to plural 'you all' by adding مْ and changing the vowel. This word is part of a question about what the people used to worship.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it present tense: sukun on first letter, kasra on middle letter, and damma at end
Added تُ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, it's addressing those who used to associate partners with Allah.
